body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: To include accurate SDK guidance in the module's doc digest, I should consult Azure Blob Storage docs on retries and metadata, then proceed to implement the ETL in-memory pipeline and run it to produce the required dict. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Blob Storage SDK retry policy exponential backoff 429 5xx metadata keys case-insensitive size limit best practices", "k": 6} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Retry guidance for Azure SDKs","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-sdk/retries"},{"title":"Azure Storage (Blob) client library retry policies","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/storage/common/retry-requests"},{"title":"Set and retrieve metadata for blobs","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/storage/blobs/storage-blob-properties-metadata#metadata"},{"title":"Azure Storage scalability and performance targets","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/storage/common/scalability-targets-standard-account"},{"title":"Optimistic concurrency with ETags","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/storage/common/storage-concurrency"},{"title":"Azure SDK general guidelines (Python) - retries/telemetry","url":"https://azure.github.io/azure-sdk/python_design.html#network-retries"}],"summary":"Azure SDKs provide built-in, configurable retry with exponential backoff for transient failures (timeouts, connection resets, HTTP 429, and 5xx). You can tune retry count and delay via client ‘RetryOptions’. Blob metadata is a set of small key-value pairs; keys are case-insensitive (returned in lowercase) and total size is limited; use metadata for lightweight tags, not large content. Use ETags/conditional headers to avoid overwriting concurrent updates."}} Now I'll implement the in-memory ETL in a single Python snippet using only the standard library. It will: generate synthetic orders (seeded), validate schema, enrich with tax/total/suspicious, compute partition paths, and build a manifest. It will also emit useful debug prints. The script will output a JSON-serializable dict matching the requested shape, including a 2–3 sentence Azure Blob SDK digest drawn from the docs. {"type":"code","code_command":"python - << 'PY' import json import random import string from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone from collections import defaultdict # ---- Module-level doc digest pulled from Azure Blob SDK guidance ---- doc_digest = ( "Azure Storage SDKs implement configurable, automatic retries with exponential backoff for transient failures " "(timeouts, connection resets, 429, and 5xx); tune max retries and delays via client RetryOptions rather than " "hand-rolling network logic. Blob metadata consists of small key–value pairs (keys are case-insensitive and " "returned lowercase, with size limits), so use it for lightweight tags and apply conditional requests (ETag) to " "avoid clobbering concurrent updates. These patterns map cleanly to cloud object-storage semantics and help ensure " "idempotent, resilient pipelines when containerized or run as jobs." ) # ---- Config ---- SEED = 1337 N_RECORDS = 250 CONTAINER = "orders" TAX_RATE = 0.085 # 8.5% BASE_TS = datetime(2024, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0, tzinfo=timezone.utc) # fixed for reproducibility # ---- Utilities ---- def iso_z(dt: datetime) -> str: return dt.replace(tzinfo=timezone.utc).isoformat().replace("+00:00", "Z") _rng = random.Random(SEED) # ---- Synthetic data generator ---- def _rand_sku(rng): return "SKU-" + ''.join(rng.choices(string.ascii_uppercase + string.digits, k=6)) def generate_orders(n: int, seed: int): rng = random.Random(seed) records = [] ts = BASE_TS for i in range(n): # Advance timestamp by 0-900 seconds to spread over hours/days deterministically ts = ts + timedelta(seconds=rng.randint(0, 900)) user_id = f"user-{rng.randint(1, 50):04d}" # Random number of items 1-5 num_items = rng.randint(1, 5) items = [] subtotal = 0.0 for _ in range(num_items): qty = rng.randint(1, 3) price = round(rng.uniform(1.0, 120.0), 2) items.append({"sku": _rand_sku(rng), "qty": qty, "price": price}) subtotal += qty * price # Introduce occasional zero/negative subtotal for edge-case validation (~2%) if rng.random() < 0.01: subtotal = 0.0 elif rng.random() < 0.01: subtotal = -round(rng.uniform(0.01, 10.0), 2) rec = { "orderId": f"ord-{i+1:06d}", "userId": user_id, "ts": iso_z(ts), "items": items, "subtotal": round(subtotal, 2), } records.append(rec) return records # ---- Schema validation ---- REQUIRED_FIELDS = { "orderId": str, "userId": str, "ts": str, # ISO-8601 with Z "items": list, # list of {sku:str, qty:int, price:float} "subtotal": (int, float), } ITEM_SCHEMA = { "sku": str, "qty": int, "price": (int, float), } def validate_iso8601_z(ts: str): # strict Zulu format try: if not ts.endswith("Z"): return False # fromisoformat can't parse 'Z', so replace with +00:00 datetime.fromisoformat(ts.replace("Z", "+00:00")) return True except Exception: return False def validate_schema(rec: dict) -> None: missing = [k for k in REQUIRED_FIELDS if k not in rec] if missing: raise ValueError(f"Missing required fields: {missing}") for k, typ in REQUIRED_FIELDS.items(): if not isinstance(rec[k], typ): raise TypeError(f"Field '{k}' must be of type {typ}, got {type(rec[k]).__name__}") # Timestamp format if not validate_iso8601_z(rec["ts"]): raise ValueError(f"Field 'ts' must be ISO-8601 Zulu string, got {rec['ts']}") # Items structure for idx, it in enumerate(rec["items"]): if not isinstance(it, dict): raise TypeError(f"Item at index {idx} must be dict, got {type(it).__name__}") for ik, ityp in ITEM_SCHEMA.items(): if ik not in it: raise ValueError(f"Item at index {idx} missing key '{ik}'") if not isinstance(it[ik], ityp): raise TypeError( f"Item '{ik}' at index {idx} must be {ityp}, got {type(it[ik]).__name__}" ) if it["qty"] <= 0: raise ValueError(f"Item at index {idx} has non-positive qty: {it['qty']}") if it["price"] < 0: raise ValueError(f"Item at index {idx} has negative price: {it['price']}") # ---- Enrichment ---- def enrich(rec: dict, tax_rate: float = TAX_RATE) -> dict: # Assumes validated schema suspicious = rec["subtotal"] <= 0 tax = round(max(rec["subtotal"], 0.0) * tax_rate, 2) total = round(rec["subtotal"] + tax, 2) out = dict(rec) out.update({ "tax": tax, "total": total, "suspicious": suspicious, }) return out # ---- Partitioning ---- def to_partition_path(container: str, rec: dict) -> str: # e.g., container/raw/day=YYYY-MM-DD/hour=HH dt = datetime.fromisoformat(rec["ts"].replace("Z", "+00:00")) return f"{container}/raw/day={dt.date().isoformat()}/hour={dt:%H}" # ---- Manifest ---- def build_manifest(records): # records are enriched and have partition keys computed externally or within here buckets = defaultdict(lambda: {"count": 0, "minTs": None, "maxTs": None}) for r in records: p = r["partitionKey"] ts = r["ts"] b = buckets[p] b["count"] += 1 if b["minTs"] is None or ts < b["minTs"]: b["minTs"] = ts if b["maxTs"] is None or ts > b["maxTs"]: b["maxTs"] = ts manifest = [] for p in sorted(buckets.keys()): b = buckets[p] manifest.append({ "partition": p, "count": b["count"], "minTs": b["minTs"], "maxTs": b["maxTs"], }) return manifest # ---- Pipeline ---- print("[ETL] Starting synthetic order generation...") raw = generate_orders(N_RECORDS, SEED) print(f"[ETL] Generated {len(raw)} records with seed={SEED}.") print("[ETL] Validating schema for all records...") for i, r in enumerate(raw): try: validate_schema(r) except Exception as e: # In a real pipeline, you'd route to DLQ; here we fail loudly. raise RuntimeError(f"Schema validation failed at index {i}: {e}") print("[ETL] Schema validation passed.") print("[ETL] Enriching records (tax, total, suspicious)...") enriched = [enrich(r) for r in raw] print("[ETL] Computing partition keys (Azure Blob-like paths)...") for r in enriched: r["partitionKey"] = to_partition_path(CONTAINER, r) partitions = sorted({r["partitionKey"] for r in enriched}) print(f"[ETL] Partition spread: {len(partitions)} unique partitions. Sample: {partitions[:5]}") print("[ETL] Building manifest (per-partition counts and time bounds)...") manifest = build_manifest(enriched) print(f"[ETL] Manifest built for {len(manifest)} partitions. First entry: {manifest[0] if manifest else None}") # Final assembly result = { "sampleRecords": enriched[:10], "partitions": partitions, "manifest": manifest, "docNotes": doc_digest, } print("[ETL] Completed in-memory ETL. Returning result dict.") print(json.dumps(result, indent=2)) PY"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"[ETL] Starting synthetic order generation... [ETL] Generated 250 records with seed=1337. [ETL] Validating schema for all records... [ETL] Schema validation passed. [ETL] Enriching records (tax, total, suspicious)... [ETL] Computing partition keys (Azure Blob-like paths)... [ETL] Partition spread: 13 unique partitions. Sample: ['orders/raw/day=2024-01-01/hour=00', 'orders/raw/day=2024-01-01/hour=01', 'orders/raw/day=2024-01-01/hour=02', 'orders/raw/day=2024-01-01/hour=03', 'orders/raw/day=2024-01-01/hour=04'] [ETL] Building manifest (per-partition counts and time bounds)... [ETL] Manifest built for 13 partitions. First entry: {'partition': 'orders/raw/day=2024-01-01/hour=00', 'count': 5, 'minTs': '2024-01-01T00:00:00Z', 'maxTs': '2024-01-01T00:41:01Z'} [ETL] Completed in-memory ETL. Returning result dict. { "sampleRecords": [ { "orderId": "ord-000001", "userId": "user-0027", "ts": "2024-01-01T00:10:55Z", "items": [ { "sku": "SKU-4W3R7E", "qty": 2, "price": 112.48 }, { "sku": "SKU-Z5Q1CC", "qty": 2, "price": 95.04 }, { "sku": "SKU-04H7C6", "qty": 3, "price": 86.7 }, { "sku": "SKU-6VQ3F7", "qty": 1, "price": 67.4 } ], "subtotal": 743.24, "tax": 63.18, "total": 806.42, "suspicious": false, "partitionKey": "orders/raw/day=2024-01-01/hour=00" }, { "orderId": "ord-000002", "userId": "user-0013", "ts": "2024-01-01T00:20:53Z", "items": [ { "sku": "SKU-Z5FC7Y", "qty": 2, "price": 85.25 }, { "sku": "SKU-8J6NZ7", "qty": 1, "price": 49.72 }, { "sku": "SKU-BF9D7Z", "qty": 1, "price": 4.81 } ], "subtotal": 224.03, "tax": 19.04, "total": 243.07, "suspicious": false, "partitionKey": "orders/raw/day=2024-01-01/hour=00" }, { "orderId": "ord-000003", "userId": "user-0019", "ts": "2024-01-01T00:29:28Z", "items": [ { "sku": "SKU-J3E4XY", "qty": 3, "price": 31.32 } ], "subtotal": 93.96, "tax": 7.99, "total": 101.95, "suspicious": false, "partitionKey": "orders/raw/day=2024-01-01/hour=00" }, { "orderId": "ord-000004", "userId": "user-0044", "ts": "2024-01-01T00:40:58Z", "items": [ { "sku": "SKU-ZO2J7Q", "qty": 1, "price": 13.27 } ], "subtotal": 13.27, "tax": 1.13, "total": 14.4, "suspicious": false, "partitionKey": "orders/raw/day=2024-01-01/hour=00" }, { "orderId": "ord-000005", "userId": "user-0042", "ts": "2024-01-01T00:41:01Z", "items": [ { "sku": "SKU-LQ6Y7S", "qty": 2, "price": 38.25 }, { "sku": "SKU-6W0ZPP", "qty": 3, "price": 3.49 }, { "sku": "SKU-EY3E1L", "qty": 3, "price": 37.13 } ], "subtotal": 209.11, "tax": 17.77, "total": 226.88, "suspicious": false, "partitionKey": body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: First, I should skim Azure Cognitive Search official guidance on field attributes and analyzers to justify field design and tokenization choices. Then I’ll implement a deterministic, config-driven in-memory pipeline in Python that fabricates 50 tickets, normalizes and tokenizes text, constructs indexable docs, and provides a simple mock search ranking by term overlap with recency tie-break. I’ll output the requested JSON structure by running the script and capturing its stdout. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Cognitive Search fields searchable filterable facetable analyzer guidance collection of strings field attributes", "k": 5} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Create a search index and define fields (Azure Cognitive Search)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/search/search-what-is-an-index"},{"title":"Field attributes: searchable, filterable, sortable, facetable, retrievable","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/search/search-what-is-an-index#fields-and-attributes"},{"title":"Analyzers, tokenizers, and filters in Azure Cognitive Search","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/search/index-add-language-analyzers"},{"title":"Data types and collections (Edm.String, Collection(Edm.String), Edm.DateTimeOffset)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/search/search-lucene-query-architecture#data-types"},{"title":"Improve filter and facet performance; avoid filterable on large text","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/search/search-howto-manage-profiles#optimize-filtering-and-faceting"}],"summary":"Azure Cognitive Search recommends using 'searchable' for full-text fields, 'filterable' and 'facetable' on low-cardinality fields (like product/category), and avoiding 'filterable' on large text to reduce index size and memory use. Use Collection(Edm.String) for tags/keywords. The Standard Lucene analyzer or language-specific analyzers handle case-folding and Unicode normalization; avoid stemming for filter fields. Date-time fields (Edm.DateTimeOffset) are commonly filterable/sortable for recency. Keep IDs as string keys and retrievable. Analyzers apply only to 'searchable' text fields."}} Now I’ll implement the deterministic, config-driven generator and search functions in a Python script, print the required JSON object, and then run it to capture output. I’ll create a small library of combos to ensure queries match, normalization via NFC/lower/trim, a simple tokenizer, and rank by term overlap (ties: newer createdAt first). {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azsearch_sim && cat > /workspace/azsearch_sim/sim.py << 'PY' import json, random, re, unicodedata from collections import Counter from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone from typing import List, Dict, Any # Config for normalization and tokenization CONFIG = { "seed": 1337, "normalization": { "nfc": True, "lower": True, "trim": True, "collapse_ws": True, }, "tokenizer": { # Use unicode word chars; later filter by min length "pattern": r"\w+", "min_len": 2, } } PRODUCTS = ["ContosoMail", "FabrikamPay", "NorthwindCRM", "TailwindERP", "AdventureDocs"] # Ten deterministic topic/phrase combos to ensure query coverage COMBOS = [ ("Password reset", "cannot reset password; code not received"), ("Billing error", "visa payment declined; duplicate charge observed"), ("Email sync", "email sync failure; imap timeout"), ("Two-factor auth", "two factor code not arriving; sms delay"), ("API timeout", "gateway timeout 504; retry later"), ("Export CSV", "export to csv uses wrong delimiter; comma vs semicolon"), ("Mobile crash", "app crashes on ios 17.1; reproduces consistently"), ("Permissions", "permission denied updating role; forbidden 403"), ("Unicode", "cafe\u0301 shows as cafe? resume re\u0301sume\u0301 not parsed"), ("Webhook", "webhook signature invalid; sha256 mismatch"), ] EXTRAS = [ "Affected users ~50.", "Happens after update v2.3.1.", "Works on WiFi, not on 4G.", "Customer is on Premium plan.", "See screenshot attached.", "Steps included in ticket.", "Region: EU-West.", "Reproduced on Android & iOS.", "Observed since yesterday.", "Escalated to Tier-2.", ] BASE_TIME = datetime(2024, 1, 1, 8, 0, 0, tzinfo=timezone.utc) def normalize_text(text: str, cfg: Dict[str, Any]) -> str: t = text if cfg["nfc"]: t = unicodedata.normalize("NFC", t) if cfg["trim"]: t = t.strip() if cfg["lower"]: t = t.lower() if cfg["collapse_ws"]: t = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", t) return t def tokenize(text: str, cfg: Dict[str, Any]) -> List[str]: t = normalize_text(text, CONFIG["normalization"]) # normalization chain toks = re.findall(cfg["pattern"], t, flags=re.UNICODE) if cfg["min_len"] and cfg["min_len"] > 1: toks = [tok for tok in toks if len(tok) >= cfg["min_len"]] return toks def fabricate_tickets(n: int = 50) -> List[Dict[str, Any]]: random.seed(CONFIG["seed"]) tickets = [] for i in range(n): tid = f"T-{i+1:04d}" # Deterministic selection to ensure coverage combo = COMBOS[i % len(COMBOS)] product = PRODUCTS[i % len(PRODUCTS)] subject, phrase = combo extra = EXTRAS[i % len(EXTRAS)] # Mix casing/punctuation/whitespace and include product marker raw_text = f" {subject} :: {phrase}! {extra} [{product}] " # Deterministic time deltas days = (3 * i + 7) % 91 minutes = (37 * i + 11) % 1440 created_at = BASE_TIME + timedelta(days=days, minutes=minutes) tickets.append({ "id": tid, "createdAt": created_at, "product": product, "text": raw_text, }) return tickets def build_index_docs(tickets: List[Dict[str, Any]]) -> List[Dict[str, Any]]: idx_docs = [] for t in tickets: norm_text = normalize_text(t["text"], CONFIG["normalization"]) toks = tokenize(norm_text, CONFIG["tokenizer"]) # already normalized counts = Counter(toks) # Unique terms sorted by (freq desc, term asc) for determinism terms_sorted = [k for k, _ in sorted(counts.items(), key=lambda kv: (-kv[1], kv[0]))] idx_docs.append({ "id": t["id"], "createdAt": t["createdAt"].strftime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"), "product": t["product"], "text": norm_text, "terms": terms_sorted, }) return idx_docs def score_doc_by_overlap(doc_counts: Counter, query_counts: Counter) -> int: score = 0 for term, qn in query_counts.items(): dn = doc_counts.get(term, 0) if dn: score += min(qn, dn) return score def search(index_docs: List[Dict[str, Any]], query: str, product_filter: str | None = None) -> Dict[str, Any]: q_tokens = tokenize(query, CONFIG["tokenizer"]) # tokenize applies normalization chain q_counts = Counter(q_tokens) considered_fields = ["text", "terms"] # Precompute doc token counts from text for ranking scored = [] for d in index_docs: if product_filter and d["product"] != product_filter: continue doc_tokens = tokenize(d["text"], CONFIG["tokenizer"]) # text is normalized in doc d_counts = Counter(doc_tokens) score = score_doc_by_overlap(d_counts, q_counts) if score > 0: matched = sorted(set(q_counts.keys()) & set(d_counts.keys())) scored.append({ "id": d["id"], "product": d["product"], "createdAt": d["createdAt"], "score": score, "matchedTerms": matched, }) # Tie-break by recency: newer createdAt first scored.sort(key=lambda x: (x["score"], x["createdAt"]), reverse=True) return { "query": query, "filter": (f"product == '{product_filter}'" if product_filter else None), "consideredFields": considered_fields, "results": scored[:5], } def field_design() -> Dict[str, Any]: return { "id": { "type": "Edm.String", "searchable": False, "filterable": True, "facetable": False, "retrievable": True, }, "createdAt": { "type": "Edm.DateTimeOffset", "searchable": False, "filterable": True, "facetable": False, "retrievable": True, }, "product": { "type": "Edm.String", "searchable": False, "filterable": True, "facetable": True, "retrievable": True, }, "text": { "type": "Edm.String", "searchable": True, "filterable": False, "facetable": False, "retrievable": True, }, "terms": { "type": "Collection(Edm.String)", "searchable": True, "filterable": False, "facetable": False, "retrievable": True, }, } def doc_notes() -> str: return ( "Fields follow Azure Cognitive Search guidance: full text is 'searchable' only (no filtering) " "to keep index size low; 'product' is low-cardinality so it's 'filterable' and 'facetable' for drilldowns; " "'createdAt' is a DateTimeOffset for filters/sorting by recency; IDs are retrievable strings. " "We used simple NFC normalization, lowercasing, and a basic word tokenizer to mimic the Standard Lucene analyzer. " "Collection(Edm.String) 'terms' can support tag-like queries or boosting; it's searchable but not filterable/facetable " "to avoid high fan-out costs. These choices align with docs recommending analyzers only on 'searchable' fields and " "keeping filters/facets on concise fields (product/category)." ) def main(): tickets = fabricate_tickets(50) idx_docs = build_index_docs(tickets) # Sample queries q1 = search(idx_docs, "reset password code not received", product_filter="ContosoMail") q2 = search(idx_docs, "billing visa charge duplicate") out = { "indexDocs": idx_docs[:5], "fieldDesign": field_design(), "searchDemo": { "example1": q1, "example2": q2, }, "docNotes": doc_notes(), } print(json.dumps(out, ensure_ascii=False, separators=(",", ":"))) if __name__ == "__main__": main() PY python3 /workspace/azsearch_sim/sim.py"}

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV,
