Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: First, I’ll ground the simulator’s assumptions in official Azure Cosmos DB docs: consistency levels (eventual vs session) and change feed ordering guarantees (per-partition ordering, no global ordering). I’ll use web search to fetch high-level docs before implementing the simulator. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Cosmos DB change feed ordering guarantees partition key range logical sequence number; Azure Cosmos DB consistency levels eventual vs session read-your-writes; Azure Cosmos DB change feed consumer ordering", "k": 6} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Introduction to Azure Cosmos DB change feed","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cosmos-db/change-feed"},{"title":"Change feed processor in Azure Cosmos DB","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cosmos-db/change-feed-processor"},{"title":"Consistency levels in Azure Cosmos DB","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cosmos-db/consistency-levels"},{"title":"Partitioning and partition keys in Azure Cosmos DB","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cosmos-db/partitioning-overview"},{"title":"Change feed design patterns and considerations","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cosmos-db/change-feed-design-patterns"},{"title":"How to read from change feed and ordering guarantees","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cosmos-db/nosql/change-feed#read-from-the-change-feed"}],"summary":"Cosmos DB change feed provides an ordered log of item mutations within a logical partition (defined by partition key). Ordering is guaranteed within a logical partition key and within a physical partition (partition key range), but there is no global ordering across partitions. The change feed is durable and can be read from the beginning or the latest. Consistency levels: eventual prioritizes availability/latency but does not guarantee read-your-writes; session consistency provides read-your-writes and monotonic reads within a session token. Consumers should not assume cross-partition ordering; they should track per-partition continuation tokens or LSNs. Partitioning guidance emphasizes stable partition keys; changing a partition key requires item recreation."}} Now I’ll implement the in-memory simulator. I’ll write a Python script that: - Generates a deterministic base collection (~30 docs) and a delta feed (~10 ops). - Validates partition key presence and stability (no updates that move partition keys). - Assigns partition key ranges by hashing userId into 4 ranges; assigns per-range LSNs. - Builds change feed envelopes showing {lsn, pkRangeId, pk, id, op}. - Replays deterministically with exactly-once semantics by deduping on (pkRangeId, lsn). - Estimates RU budget using a simple table: insert=10, update=5, delete=2. - Emits required JSON: {'finalCollection': first 8 docs, 'feedEnvelopes': first 8 envelopes, 'ruEstimate': number, 'assumptions': digest}. I’ll code it under /workspace/cdc_sim/cdc_sim.py and execute it. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/cdc_sim && cat > /workspace/cdc_sim/cdc_sim.py << 'PY' import json import random import hashlib from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict from typing import Dict, List, Optional, Tuple, Any # Deterministic seed for repeatability random.seed(1337) # Operation and item shapes OPS = ["insert", "update", "delete"] @dataclass class Item: id: str userId: str # partition key body: Dict[str, Any] @dataclass class Change: op: str # 'insert' | 'update' | 'delete' id: str userId: str body: Optional[Dict[str, Any]] = None @dataclass class Envelope: lsn: int # logical seq number within pkRangeId pkRangeId: str # simulated physical partition range id pk: str # logical partition key (userId) id: str op: str def pk_range_for(user_id: str, num_ranges: int = 4) -> str: # Use stable hash of userId to map to a range [0..num_ranges-1] h = int(hashlib.sha1(user_id.encode("utf-8")).hexdigest(), 16) return str(h % num_ranges) def make_base_collection(n_users: int = 6, n_docs: int = 30) -> Dict[Tuple[str, str], Item]: users = [f"user-{i}" for i in range(1, n_users + 1)] col: Dict[Tuple[str, str], Item] = {} for i in range(1, n_docs + 1): uid = random.choice(users) doc_id = f"doc-{i:03d}" body = { "type": random.choice(["note", "todo", "msg"]), "value": random.randint(1, 100), "updatedAt": 0, } col[(doc_id, uid)] = Item(id=doc_id, userId=uid, body=body) return col def pick_existing_key(col: Dict[Tuple[str, str], Item]) -> Tuple[str, str]: return random.choice(list(col.keys())) def make_delta(col: Dict[Tuple[str, str], Item], n_ops: int = 10) -> List[Change]: # Use existing users and ids to create realistic ops; include some inserts for new ids users = list({userId for (_, userId) in col.keys()}) existing_ids = {doc_id for (doc_id, _) in col.keys()} delta: List[Change] = [] for i in range(n_ops): op = random.choices(OPS, weights=[0.4, 0.4, 0.2])[0] if op == "insert": uid = random.choice(users) # Ensure a new ID 80% of the time; 20% insert duplicate to test idempotency skip if random.random() < 0.8: doc_id = f"doc-new-{i:02d}" else: doc_id = random.choice(list(existing_ids)) body = { "type": random.choice(["note", "todo", "msg"]), "value": random.randint(100, 200), "updatedAt": 1, } delta.append(Change(op=op, id=doc_id, userId=uid, body=body)) elif op == "update": doc_id, uid = pick_existing_key(col) # Occasionally attempt an update that tries to change partition key (should be invalid) if random.random() < 0.2: # Pick a different userId to test PK stability enforcement other_users = [u for u in users if u != uid] uid_bad = random.choice(other_users) if other_users else uid body = { "value": random.randint(200, 300), "updatedAt": 1, } delta.append(Change(op=op, id=doc_id, userId=uid_bad, body=body)) else: body = { "value": random.randint(200, 300), "updatedAt": 1, } delta.append(Change(op=op, id=doc_id, userId=uid, body=body)) else: # delete doc_id, uid = pick_existing_key(col) delta.append(Change(op=op, id=doc_id, userId=uid, body=None)) return delta class ValidationError(Exception): pass def validate_delta(base: Dict[Tuple[str, str], Item], delta: List[Change]) -> None: """ Enforce presence and stability of partition key (userId). - All ops must have userId and id. - update must reference existing (id, userId) in the evolving state; cannot change userId. - insert must not collide with an existing (id, userId) unless it's an identical idempotent re-insert with same body (we'll disallow for simplicity and handle idempotency at replay layer). - delete must reference existing (id, userId) in the evolving state at the moment of deletion. We validate by simulating state transitions. """ state: Dict[Tuple[str, str], Item] = dict(base) for idx, ch in enumerate(delta): if not ch.userId or not ch.id: raise ValidationError(f"Change #{idx} missing id or userId") key = (ch.id, ch.userId) if ch.op == "insert": if key in state: # Allow as a logical duplicate candidate; still considered invalid per strict validator raise ValidationError(f"Change #{idx} insert collides with existing item {key}") if ch.body is None: raise ValidationError(f"Change #{idx} insert missing body") state[key] = Item(id=ch.id, userId=ch.userId, body=ch.body) elif ch.op == "update": # PK stability: must exist with same userId if key not in state: raise ValidationError(f"Change #{idx} update targets non-existent item {key} (or PK changed)") if ch.body is None: raise ValidationError(f"Change #{idx} update missing body") # apply patch-like merge; overwrite provided fields in body new_body = dict(state[key].body) new_body.update(ch.body) state[key] = Item(id=ch.id, userId=ch.userId, body=new_body) elif ch.op == "delete": if key not in state: raise ValidationError(f"Change #{idx} delete targets non-existent item {key}") del state[key] else: raise ValidationError(f"Change #{idx} unknown op {ch.op}") def assign_envelopes(delta: List[Change], num_ranges: int = 4) -> List[Envelope]: # Maintain per-range LSN counters counters: Dict[str, int] = {str(i): 0 for i in range(num_ranges)} envelopes: List[Envelope] = [] for ch in delta: r = pk_range_for(ch.userId, num_ranges) counters[r] += 1 envelopes.append(Envelope(lsn=counters[r], pkRangeId=r, pk=ch.userId, id=ch.id, op=ch.op)) return envelopes def replay_exactly_once(base: Dict[Tuple[str, str], Item], delta: List[Change], envelopes: List[Envelope]) -> Dict[Tuple[str, str], Item]: # Build a map from change to its unique opKey (pkRangeId, lsn) for idempotency # Because we created envelopes in order with per-range incremental LSNs op_keys: List[Tuple[str, int]] = [(env.pkRangeId, env.lsn) for env in envelopes] # Compose a list of (opKey, change) preserving original delta order ops_with_keys = list(zip(op_keys, delta)) # State and applied set state: Dict[Tuple[str, str], Item] = dict(base) applied = set() # set of (pkRangeId, lsn) for (op_key, ch) in ops_with_keys: if op_key in applied: continue # exactly-once applied.add(op_key) key = (ch.id, ch.userId) if ch.op == "insert": # Upsert-style behavior: if already exists with same pk, treat as idempotent insert and replace body # (Cosmos createItem would fail on duplicate; but change feed replays should not double apply.) if ch.body is None: continue state[key] = Item(id=ch.id, userId=ch.userId, body=dict(ch.body)) elif ch.op == "update": # Patch-like merge; if missing (due to prior delete not applied yet), create? We'll enforce existence if key not in state: # In an at-least-once delivery system, an update before insert would be problematic; # for this simulator we ensure delta validation prevents it. If encountered, skip. continue newb = dict(state[key].body) newb.update(ch.body or {}) state[key] = Item(id=ch.id, userId=ch.userId, body=newb) elif ch.op == "delete": state.pop(key, None) return state def estimate_ru(delta: List[Change]) -> int: # Simple table; insert > update > delete cost = {"insert": 10, "update": 5, "delete": 2} return sum(cost.get(ch.op, 5) for ch in delta) def first_n_docs(state: Dict[Tuple[str, str], Item], n: int = 8) -> List[Dict[str, Any]]: # Deterministic order: sort by (userId, id) items = list(state.values()) items.sort(key=lambda it: (it.userId, it.id)) return [asdict(it) for it in items[:n]] def first_n_env(envelopes: List[Envelope], n: int = 8) -> List[Dict[str, Any]]: # Show minimal envelope fields out = [] for env in envelopes[:n]: out.append({ "lsn": env.lsn, "pkRangeId": env.pkRangeId, "pk": env.pk, "id": env.id, "op": env.op, }) return out def build_assumptions_digest() -> str: # Three bullet digest (string) re: eventual vs session and ordering expectations bullets = [ "- Session consistency provides read-your-writes and monotonic reads within a session (via session tokens); eventual consistency trades this for lower latency/higher availability and may surface changes later or out of order across partitions.", "- Change feed ordering is guaranteed within a logical partition key (and within a physical partition key range) but there is no global cross-partition ordering; consumers must not assume a total order across ranges.", "- Consumers should track per-partition continuation (e.g., LSN/etag/session token) and implement idempotent processing; replays may deliver duplicates or gaps out of order across partitions under at-least-once semantics.", ] return "\n".join(bullets) def main(): base = make_base_collection(n_users=6, n_docs=30) # Create a candidate delta and loop until it passes validator to ensure realism attempts = 0 while True: attempts += 1 delta = make_delta(base, n_ops=10) try: validate_delta(base, delta) break except ValidationError: if attempts > 50: raise continue envelopes = assign_envelopes(delta, num_ranges=4) # Apply replay twice to verify exactly-once behavior state_after_once = replay_exactly_once(base, delta, envelopes) state_after_twice = replay_exactly_once(state_after_once, delta, envelopes) assert len(state_after_once) == len(state_after_twice), "Idempotency violated: size differs" # Spot-check equality by comparing serialized sorted keys def ser(state): return sorted(((k[0], k[1], json.dumps(v.body, sort_keys=True)) for k, v in state.items())) assert ser(state_after_once) == ser(state_after_twice), "Idempotency violated: contents differ" ru = estimate_ru(delta) assumptions = build_assumptions_digest() out = { "finalCollection": first_n_docs(state_after_once, 8), "feedEnvelopes": first_n_env(envelopes, 8), "ruEstimate": ru, "assumptions": assumptions, } print(json.dumps(out, indent=2)) if __name__ == "__main__": main() PY python3 /workspace/cdc_sim/cdc_sim.py"}

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: First, I’ll skim Azure Event Hubs docs to capture authoritative wording about throughput units vs partitions and the role of checkpointing, so the ops note is accurate. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Event Hubs throughput units partitions checkpointing consumer groups offsets", "k": 5} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Throughput units and scaling in Azure Event Hubs","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/event-hubs/event-hubs-scalability#throughput-units"},{"title":"Event Hubs partitions and consumer groups","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/event-hubs/event-hubs-features#partitions"},{"title":"Checkpointing with EventProcessorClient (Event Hubs)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/event-hubs/event-processor-balance-partition-load#checkpointing"},{"title":"Event Hubs concepts","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/event-hubs/event-hubs-about"},{"title":"Best practices for Event Hubs consumers","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/event-hubs/event-hubs-performance-guide#consumers"}],"summary":"Docs state that throughput units (TUs) are capacity allocations that provide approximately 1 MB/s ingress and 2 MB/s egress per TU, and adding more partitions increases parallelism but does not raise the TU cap. Checkpointing persists the last processed position per consumer group and partition to durable storage so that on restart consumers resume from the last checkpoint, minimizing duplicate processing."}} Now I’ll implement a fully in-memory, deterministic simulator in a single Python script run via stdin. It will: generate 500 records; assign partitions using a deterministic hash(deviceId) mod 4; model two consumer groups with per-partition checkpoints; simulate a partial read, persist checkpoints, and restart to finish; compute per-partition stats and sample 5-minute window p50/p95 CPU aggregates. I’ll print a compact JSON result. No files will be written. {"type":"code","code_command":"python3 - << 'PY' import json, math from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone # Deterministic generator settings P = 4 start = datetime(2025,1,1,0,0,0,tzinfo=timezone.utc) interval = timedelta(seconds=30) num_records = 500 devices = [f"device{str(i).zfill(2)}" for i in range(1,21)] # 20 devices # Deterministic hash: extract numeric suffix and mod P # (Simulates hash(deviceId) mod P deterministically for this demo) def hash_device(device_id: str) -> int: n = int(device_id[-2:]) return n % P # Deterministic CPU generator: base per device + mild drift and bounded noise # bounded noise is a simple function of (record_index, device_index) — no RNG dev_index = {d:i for i,d in enumerate(devices)} def gen_cpu(idx:int, device_id:str) -> float: di = dev_index[device_id] base = 25 + di * 2.0 # 25..63 across 20 devices drift = (idx % 10) * 0.6 # 0..5.4 sawtooth noise = ((idx*17 + di*13) % 21) - 10 # integer in [-10,10] v = base + drift + noise if v < 0: v = 0.0 if v > 100: v = 100.0 return round(v, 1) # Generate records records = [] for i in range(num_records): device = devices[i % len(devices)] ts = start + i*interval cpu = gen_cpu(i, device) pid = hash_device(device) records.append({ 'idx': i, 'deviceId': device, 'ts': ts, 'cpu': cpu, 'partitionId': pid, }) # Partition streams with stable ordering (arrival order) partitions = {p: [] for p in range(P)} for r in records: partitions[r['partitionId']].append(r) # Assign per-partition offsets for p in range(P): for off, r in enumerate(partitions[p]): r['offset'] = off # Two consumer groups with per-partition checkpoints consumer_groups = ['cgA', 'cgB'] checkpoints = {cg: {p: -1 for p in range(P)} for cg in consumer_groups} # Simulate processing with partial pass and restart # Pass 1: cgA processes ~50% of each partition, cgB processes ~33% def process_until_fraction(cg:str, frac:float): for p in range(P): stream = partitions[p] start_off = checkpoints[cg][p] + 1 target_exclusive = max(start_off, int(math.floor(len(stream) * frac))) if target_exclusive > len(stream): target_exclusive = len(stream) if start_off < target_exclusive: # process stream[start_off:target_exclusive] checkpoints[cg][p] = target_exclusive - 1 process_until_fraction('cgA', 0.5) process_until_fraction('cgB', 1.0/3.0) # Simulate restart: complete processing to end process_until_fraction('cgA', 1.0) process_until_fraction('cgB', 1.0) # Per-partition stats parts_stats = {} for p in range(P): stream = partitions[p] count = len(stream) devs = sorted({r['deviceId'] for r in stream}) first_ts = stream[0]['ts'].isoformat().replace('+00:00','Z') if stream else None last_ts = stream[-1]['ts'].isoformat().replace('+00:00','Z') if stream else None parts_stats[str(p)] = { 'count': count, 'uniqueDevices': len(devs), 'offsetEnd': count-1 if count>0 else -1, 'firstTs': first_ts, 'lastTs': last_ts, } # Windowed p50/p95 per partition for 5-minute windows (floor to 5-min) WINDOW = 300 # seconds def window_floor_iso(ts: datetime) -> str: epoch = int(ts.timestamp()) wstart = epoch - (epoch % WINDOW) return datetime.fromtimestamp(wstart, tz=timezone.utc).isoformat().replace('+00:00','Z') from collections import defaultdict win_vals = {p: defaultdict(list) for p in range(P)} for p in range(P): for r in partitions[p]: w = window_floor_iso(r['ts']) win_vals[p][w].append(r['cpu']) def q_linear(vals, q): if not vals: return None xs = sorted(vals) n = len(xs) if n == 1: return float(xs[0]) pos = (n - 1) * q lo = int(math.floor(pos)) hi = int(math.ceil(pos)) if lo == hi: return float(xs[lo]) frac = pos - lo return float(round(xs[lo] + (xs[hi] - xs[lo]) * frac, 1)) windowAgg = {} for p in range(P): entries = [] for w in sorted(win_vals[p].keys())[:2]: # first two windows per partition vals = win_vals[p][w] entries.append({ 'windowStart': w, 'count': len(vals), 'p50': round(q_linear(vals, 0.5), 1) if vals else None, 'p95': round(q_linear(vals, 0.95), 1) if vals else None, }) windowAgg[str(p)] = entries # Final checkpoint table ck_list = [] for cg in consumer_groups: for p in range(P): ck_list.append({ 'consumerGroup': cg, 'partitionId': p, 'offset': checkpoints[cg][p] }) # Ops notes (short, will be augmented in the final narrative) opsNotes = ( 'Partitioning by deviceId via hash(deviceId) mod 4 preserves per-device order and spreads load; ' 'each consumer group tracks independent per-partition checkpoints to isolate progress.' ) result = { 'partitions': parts_stats, 'checkpoints': ck_list, 'windowAgg': windowAgg, 'opsNotes': opsNotes } print(json.dumps(result, separators=(",",":"))) PY"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"{"partitions":{"0":{"count":125,"uniqueDevices":5,"offsetEnd":124,"firstTs":"2025-01-01T00:01:30Z","lastTs":"2025-01-01T04:09:30Z"},"1":{"count":125,"uniqueDevices":5,"offsetEnd":124,"firstTs":"2025-01-01T00:00:00Z","lastTs":"2025-01-01T04:08:00Z"},"2":{"count":125,"uniqueDevices":5,"offsetEnd":124,"firstTs":"2025-01-01T00:00:30Z","lastTs":"2025-01-01T04:08:30Z"},"3":{"count":125,"uniqueDevices":5,"offsetEnd":124,"firstTs":"2025-01-01T00:01:00Z","lastTs":"2025-01-01T04:09:00Z"}},"checkpoints":[{"consumerGroup":"cgA","partitionId":0,"offset":124},{"consumerGroup":"cgA","partitionId":1,"offset":124},{"consumerGroup":"cgA","partitionId":2,"offset":124},{"consumerGroup":"cgA","partitionId":3,"offset":124},{"consumerGroup":"cgB","partitionId":0,"offset":124},{"consumerGroup":"cgB","partitionId":1,"offset":124},{"consumerGroup":"cgB","partitionId":2,"offset":124},{"consumerGroup":"cgB","partitionId":3,"offset":124}],"windowAgg":{"0":[{"windowStart":"2025-01-01T00:00:00Z","count":10,"p50":43.0,"p95":57.3},{"windowStart":"2025-01-01T00:05:00Z","count":10,"p50":45.2,"p95":59.5}],"1":[{"windowStart":"2025-01-01T00:00:00Z","count":10,"p50":41.4,"p95":56.9},{"windowStart":"2025-01-01T00:05:00Z","count":10,"p50":43.6,"p95":58.8}],"2":[{"windowStart":"2025-01-01T00:00:00Z","count":10,"p50":41.9,"p95":57.0},{"windowStart":"2025-01-01T00:05:00Z","count":10,"p50":44.1,"p95":59.1}],"3":[{"windowStart":"2025-01-01T00:00:00Z","count":10,"p50":42.5,"p95":57.6},{"windowStart":"2025-01-01T00:05:00Z","count":10,"p50":44.7,"p95":59.8}]},"opsNotes":"Partitioning by deviceId via hash(deviceId) mod 4 preserves per-device order and spreads load; each consumer group tracks independent per-partition checkpoints to isolate progress."}","stderr":""} Now I’ll return the final result: the JSON object with partitions, checkpoints, window aggregates, and ops notes enhanced with the two-sentence doc note from Event Hubs about throughput units vs partitions and checkpointing’s effect on duplicates. {"partitions": {"0": {"count": 125, "uniqueDevices": 5, "offsetEnd": 124, "firstTs": "2025-01-01T00:01:30Z", "lastTs": "2025-01-01T04:09:30Z"}, "1": {"count": "2025-01-01T00:05:00Z", "count": 10, "p50": 43.6, "p95": 58.8}], "2": [{"windowStart": "2025-01-01T00:00:00Z", "count": 10, "p50": 41.9, "p95": 57.0}, {"windowStart": "2025-01-01T00:05:00Z", "count": 10, "p50": 44.1, "p95": 59.1}], "3": [{"windowStart": "2025-01-01T00:00:00Z", "count": 10, "p50": 42.5, "p95": 57.6}, {"windowStart": "2025-01-01T00:05:00Z", "count": 10, "p50": 44.7, "p95": 59.8}]}, "opsNotes": "Partitioning by deviceId using hash(deviceId) mod 4 preserves per-device ordering and spreads load evenly; each consumer group maintains independent checkpoints per partition to isolate progress and enable parallel consumption. Azure Event Hubs docs note that throughput units (TUs) govern ingress/egress capacity (about 1 MB/s in and 2 MB/s out per TU) and are independent of the number of partitions—more partitions increase parallelism but don’t raise the TU cap. Persisting checkpoints per consumer group and partition lets processors resume from the last offset after restarts, avoiding re-reading and reprocessing events, which reduces duplicate work."} Notes - Implementation is entirely in-memory and deterministic (fixed start time, interval, device set, and CPU function). No external systems or files are used. - Partition assignment is a deterministic hash(deviceId) mod 4, yielding five devices per partition and 125 events per partition (offsetEnd 124). Two-pass read simulates partial processing and restart; final checkpoints show both consumer groups completed all partitions. - 5-minute windows are defined by flooring event timestamps to 5-minute boundaries; shown p50/p95 values are sample aggregates for the first two windows per partition. - Doc sources skimmed: Throughput units and scaling; partitions and consumer groups; and checkpointing guidance (EventProcessorClient) on learn.microsoft.com for Azure Event Hubs.

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: I'll implement the in-memory packaging by writing a small Python snippet that synthetically generates binary data, trains a minimal logistic regression via gradient descent, computes metrics, assembles an evaluation bundle object, runs a promotion gate with thresholds and drift checks, and prints a JSON payload containing the bundle, samplePredictions, and gateDecision. After that, I’ll consult Azure ML docs on registries/endpoints to distill two sentences about why stable I/O contracts reduce deployment risk. {"type":"code","code_command":"python3 - << 'PY' import json, math, random, hashlib try: import numpy as np except Exception: np = None SEED = 7 random.seed(SEED) # Helper functions def sigmoid(z): if isinstance(z, float) or isinstance(z, int): return 1.0/(1.0+math.exp(-z)) return 1.0/(1.0+np.exp(-z)) def train_logreg_gd(X, y, lr=0.1, epochs=250, l2=0.0): n, d = X.shape w = np.zeros(d) b = 0.0 for _ in range(epochs): z = X.dot(w) + b p = sigmoid(z) # gradient err = (p - y) gw = (X.T.dot(err)) / n + l2 * w gb = err.mean() w -= lr * gw b -= lr * gb return w, b def binary_metrics(y_true, y_pred): # y_true, y_pred are 0/1 arrays tp = int(((y_true==1) & (y_pred==1)).sum()) tn = int(((y_true==0) & (y_pred==0)).sum()) fp = int(((y_true==0) & (y_pred==1)).sum()) fn = int(((y_true==1) & (y_pred==0)).sum()) acc = (tp + tn) / max(1, len(y_true)) prec = tp / max(1, (tp + fp)) rec = tp / max(1, (tp + fn)) if prec + rec == 0: f1 = 0.0 else: f1 = 2 * prec * rec / (prec + rec) return { 'accuracy': acc, 'precision': prec, 'recall': rec, 'f1': f1, 'tp': tp, 'tn': tn, 'fp': fp, 'fn': fn, } # 1) Generate deterministic synthetic binary classification data n = 1000 d = 6 rng = np.random.default_rng(SEED) X = rng.normal(0.0, 1.0, size=(n, d)) true_w = rng.normal(0.0, 1.0, size=(d,)) true_b = 0.15 # Linear signal + a bit of structured feature influence lin = X.dot(true_w) + true_b + 0.5*X[:,0] - 0.25*X[:,1] probs = sigmoid(lin) y = (rng.uniform(0,1,size=n) < probs).astype(int) # Deterministic split (shuffle with fixed seed, then split) idx = np.arange(n) rng.shuffle(idx) train_size = int(0.8*n) train_idx = idx[:train_size] test_idx = idx[train_size:] X_train, y_train = X[train_idx], y[train_idx] X_test, y_test = X[test_idx], y[test_idx] # 2) Train a tiny logistic regression via gradient descent w, b = train_logreg_gd(X_train, y_train, lr=0.1, epochs=300, l2=0.001) # 3) Compute metrics test_probs = sigmoid(X_test.dot(w) + b) test_pred = (test_probs >= 0.5).astype(int) metrics = binary_metrics(y_test, test_pred) metrics['split'] = { 'train': int(X_train.shape[0]), 'test': int(X_test.shape[0]), 'positive_rate_train': float(y_train.mean()), 'positive_rate_test': float(y_test.mean()), } # 4) Drift sanity check: compare mean feature vector (train vs test) train_mean = X_train.mean(axis=0) test_mean = X_test.mean(axis=0) mean_diff = test_mean - train_mean mean_l2 = float(np.linalg.norm(mean_diff)) per_feature_diff = {f'f{i}': float(mean_diff[i]) for i in range(d)} # 5) Build evaluation bundle feature_names = [f'f{i}' for i in range(d)] code_version = 'v0.1.0' # data hash on raw arrays (deterministic) — still effectively a placeholder used for traceability m = hashlib.sha256() m.update(X.tobytes()); m.update(y.tobytes()) data_hash = 'sha256:' + m.hexdigest() artifacts = { 'coefficients': [float(x) for x in w], 'intercept': float(b), 'featureMeans': { 'train': [float(x) for x in train_mean], 'test': [float(x) for x in test_mean], }, 'drift': { 'meanL2': mean_l2, 'perFeatureDiff': per_feature_diff } } interface = { 'input': { 'features': [{'name': n, 'type': 'float'} for n in feature_names] }, 'output': { 'probability': {'type': 'float', 'range': [0.0, 1.0]}, 'label': {'type': 'int', 'values': [0, 1]}, 'threshold': 0.5 } } bundle = { 'codeVersion': code_version, 'dataHash': data_hash, 'metrics': {k: (float(v) if isinstance(v, (int, float)) else v) for k, v in metrics.items()}, 'artifacts': artifacts, 'interface': interface } # 6) Promotion gate thresholds = { 'min_accuracy': 0.80, 'min_f1': 0.80, 'max_driftL2': 0.50 } checks = [] checks.append({'name': 'accuracy>=min_accuracy', 'ok': metrics['accuracy'] >= thresholds['min_accuracy'], 'value': float(metrics['accuracy']), 'threshold': thresholds['min_accuracy']}) checks.append({'name': 'f1>=min_f1', 'ok': metrics['f1'] >= thresholds['min_f1'], 'value': float(metrics['f1']), 'threshold': thresholds['min_f1']}) checks.append({'name': 'drift_meanL2<=max_driftL2', 'ok': mean_l2 <= thresholds['max_driftL2'], 'value': float(mean_l2), 'threshold': thresholds['max_driftL2']}) decision = 'PASS' if all(c['ok'] for c in checks) else 'FAIL' reasons = [] for c in checks: if not c['ok']: reasons.append(f"Check failed: {c['name']} (value={c['value']}, threshold={c['threshold']})") if decision == 'PASS': reasons.append('All thresholds satisfied; metrics meet or exceed requirements and drift within bounds.') gate_decision = { 'decision': decision, 'thresholds': thresholds, 'checks': checks, 'reasons': reasons } # 7) Sample predictions (first 10 test rows) sample = [] for i in range(min(10, len(test_idx))): ridx = test_idx[i] sample.append({ 'id': f'test_{i}', 'prob': float(test_probs[i]), 'label': int(test_pred[i]), 'y_true': int(y_test[i]) }) result = { 'bundle': bundle, 'samplePredictions': sample, 'gateDecision': gate_decision } print(json.dumps(result, indent=2)) PY"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"{ "bundle": { "codeVersion": "v0.1.0", "dataHash": "sha256:7a9c0e0c7a1f87cd2d6d7b0a0e4c6d8e63d0f13afb71fdee689e7d1c4ea7aa0e", "metrics": { "accuracy": 0.8825, "precision": 0.8732, "recall": 0.8913, "f1": 0.8822, "tp": 89, "tn": 88, "fp": 13, "fn": 10, "split": { "train": 800, "test": 200, "positive_rate_train": 0.50375, "positive_rate_test": 0.495 } }, "artifacts": { "coefficients": [ 0.7154, -0.4021, 0.1578, 0.6289, -0.0845, 0.2711 ], "intercept": 0.0327, "featureMeans": { "train": [ -0.0143, 0.0098, -0.0161, 0.0105, -0.0047, 0.0269 ], "test": [ 0.0047, -0.0141, 0.0234, -0.0128, 0.0061, -0.0192 ] }, "drift": { "meanL2": 0.072, "perFeatureDiff": { "f0": 0.019, "f1": -0.0239, "f2": 0.0395, "f3": -0.0233, "f4": 0.0108, "f5": -0.0461 } } }, "interface": { "input": { "features": [ { "name": "f0", "type": "float" }, { "name": "f1", "type": "float" }, { "name": "f2", "type": "float" }, { "name": "f3", "type": "float" }, { "name": "f4", "type": "float" }, { "name": "f5", "type": "float" } ] }, "output": { "probability": { "type": "float", "range": [ 0.0, 1.0 ] }, "label": { "type": "int", "values": [ 0, 1 ] }, "threshold": 0.5 } } }, "samplePredictions": [ { "id": "test_0", "prob": 0.761, "label": 1, "y_true": 1 }, { "id": "test_1", "prob": 0.622, "label": 1, "y_true": 1 }, { "id": "test_2", "prob": 0.433, "label": 0, "y_true": 0 }, { "id": "test_3", "prob": 0.524, "label": 1, "y_true": 1 }, { "id": "test_4", "prob": 0.287, "label": 0, "y_true": 0 }, { "id": "test_5", "prob": 0.845, "label": 1, "y_true": 1 }, { "id": "test_6", "prob": 0.409, "label": 0, "y_true": 0 }, { "id": "test_7", "prob": 0.551, "label": 1, "y_true": 1 }, { "id": "test_8", "prob": 0.194, "label": 0, "y_true": 0 }, { "id": "test_9", "prob": 0.674, "label": 1, "y_true": 1 } ], "gateDecision": { "decision": "PASS", "thresholds": { "min_accuracy": 0.8, "min_f1": 0.8, "max_driftL2": 0.5 }, "checks": [ { "name": "accuracy>=min_accuracy", "ok": true, "value": 0.8825, "threshold": 0.8 }, { "name": "f1>=min_f1", "ok": true, "value": 0.8822, "threshold": 0.8 }, { "name": "drift_meanL2<=max_driftL2", "ok": true, "value": 0.072, "threshold": 0.5 } ], "reasons": [ "All thresholds satisfied; metrics meet or exceed requirements and drift within bounds." ] } } ","stderr":""} Now I want to ground the two-sentence summary by checking Azure ML docs for registries and endpoints to ensure terminology is accurate. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning registry and endpoint concepts stable input output contracts model versioning deployments", "k": 5} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"What are Azure Machine Learning registries?","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-registries"},{"title":"Deploy and manage models with managed online endpoints","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-endpoints-online"},{"title":"Model versioning and promotion across workspaces with registries","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-share-models-registries"},{"title":"Azure ML deployment best practices","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-deploy-and-where"},{"title":"MLflow models and inferencing in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-deploy-mlflow-models"}],"summary":"Azure ML registries provide organization-wide, versioned storage of models, environments, and components for reuse across workspaces.
