body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

Then generate 100 synthetic rows, validate per type rules with coercion warnings, and produce partitions and copyHints. Finally, prepare notes with 2 sentences citing common pitfalls from docs.</think>

Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: I'll generate synthetic event data and compute paths, lifecycle tiers, and conformance entirely in a Python script, then return the resulting JSON. This keeps everything "in memory" and deterministic. {"type":"code","code_command":"python - << 'PY' import random, hashlib, json, re from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone random.seed(42) now = datetime.now(timezone.utc) # Synthetic config USERS = [f"user{str(i).zfill(3)}" for i in range(1, 31)] EVENT_TYPES = ["login","view","click","add_to_cart","purchase","logout"] N_EVENTS = 250 BUCKETS = 32 # Helpers def ts_iso(dt: datetime) -> str: return dt.replace(microsecond=0).isoformat().replace('+00:00','Z') def user_bucket(user_id: str, buckets: int = BUCKETS) -> int: h = hashlib.md5(user_id.encode('utf-8')).hexdigest() return int(h, 16) % buckets SAFE_CHARS = re.compile(r"[^A-Za-z0-9_.\-]") def safe(s: str) -> str: return SAFE_CHARS.sub('_', s) # Generate synthetic events across last ~75 days for varied lifecycle records = [] for i in range(N_EVENTS): user = random.choice(USERS) et = random.choice(EVENT_TYPES) # skew toward recent days to ensure some HOT if random.random() < 0.55: days_back = random.randint(0, 5) # very recent elif random.random() < 0.85: days_back = random.randint(6, 30) # warm else: days_back = random.randint(31, 75) # cold sec_back = random.randint(0, 24*3600 - 1) dt = now - timedelta(days=days_back, seconds=sec_back) records.append({ 'userId': user, 'ts': ts_iso(dt), 'eventType': et, 'dt': dt, }) # Proposed hierarchical namespace layout # /adls/events/zone=/year=YYYY/month=MM/day=DD/eventType=/user_bucket=NN/part-YYYYMMDDTHHMMSSZ_user-.json def map_record_to_path(rec, zone='raw'): dt = rec['dt'] y = dt.year m = f"{dt.month:02d}" d = f"{dt.day:02d}" et = safe(rec['eventType']) uid = rec['userId'] b = user_bucket(uid) filename = f"part-{dt.strftime('%Y%m%dT%H%M%SZ')}_user-{safe(uid)}.json" return f"/adls/events/zone={zone}/year={y}/month={m}/day={d}/eventType={et}/user_bucket={b:02d}/{filename}" paths = [map_record_to_path(r, 'raw') for r in records] # Build partition stats (directory without filename) from collections import defaultdict part_stats = defaultdict(lambda: { 'count': 0, 'lastEventTs': None, 'dateKey': None, }) for rec, p in zip(records, paths): # dir is path without trailing filename dir_path = '/'.join(p.split('/')[:-1]) st = part_stats[dir_path] st['count'] += 1 if (st['lastEventTs'] is None) or (rec['dt'] > st['lastEventTs']): st['lastEventTs'] = rec['dt'] # date key by partition date (year/month/day segments) comps = [c for c in dir_path.split('/') if c] # comps = ['adls','events','zone=..','year=YYYY','month=MM','day=DD','eventType=..','user_bucket=..'] year = int(comps[3].split('=')[1]) month = int(comps[4].split('=')[1]) day = int(comps[5].split('=')[1]) st['dateKey'] = datetime(year, month, day, tzinfo=timezone.utc) # Lifecycle advisor # hot: 0-3 days old; warm: 4-30; cold: >30 lifecycle = [] for part, st in sorted(part_stats.items()): days_old = (now.date() - st['dateKey'].date()).days if days_old <= 3: tier = 'hot'; storage = 'Hot' elif days_old <= 30: tier = 'warm'; storage = 'Cool' else: tier = 'cold'; storage = 'Archive' lifecycle.append({ 'partition': part, 'count': st['count'], 'lastEventTs': ts_iso(st['lastEventTs']), 'recencyDays': days_old, 'tier': tier, 'suggestedStorageTier': storage, }) # Compliance checker # Expected partition directory shape (no filename): # /adls/events/zone=/year=YYYY/month=MM/day=DD/eventType=/user_bucket=NN/ expected_keys = ['zone','year','month','day','eventType','user_bucket'] YEAR_RE = re.compile(r'^year=(\d{4})$') MONTH_RE = re.compile(r'^month=(0[1-9]|1[0-2])$') DAY_RE = re.compile(r'^day=(0[1-9]|[12]\d|3[01])$') ZONE_RE = re.compile(r'^zone=(raw|curated)$') EVT_RE = re.compile(r'^eventType=([A-Za-z0-9_.\-]+)$') BUCKET_RE = re.compile(r'^user_bucket=(0\d|[12]\d|3[01])$') # 00-31 # Build a candidate partition list: some valid ones from data + some intentionally invalid valid_partitions = list(part_stats.keys()) valid_partitions_sample = sorted(valid_partitions)[:8] # Counts for valid partitions counts = {p: part_stats[p]['count'] for p in valid_partitions} # Add invalid examples invalids = [ # empty directory (no files) '/adls/events/zone=raw/year=2025/month=09/day=01/eventType=click/user_bucket=01', # missing eventType '/adls/events/zone=raw/year=2025/month=09/day=02/user_bucket=02', # non-zero-padded month & bucket '/adls/events/zone=raw/year=2025/month=9/day=07/eventType=view/user_bucket=3', # missing zone '/adls/events/year=2025/month=09/day=03/eventType=login/user_bucket=01', ] candidate = valid_partitions_sample + invalids # Ensure they end with trailing slash for directory semantics candidate = [p if p.endswith('/') else p + '/' for p in candidate] # Attach counts (0 for unknown) part_counts = {p: counts.get(p.rstrip('/'), 0) for p in candidate} def check_partition(part: str, file_count: int): issues = [] comps = [c for c in part.split('/') if c] if len(comps) < 2 or comps[0] != 'adls' or comps[1] != 'events': issues.append('bad_prefix') # continue further checks cautiously # Expect exact 8 components: adls, events, and 6 key=value parts if len(comps) != 8: issues.append('bad_depth') # Map last components kv = {} key_parts = comps[2:8] for seg in key_parts: if '=' not in seg: issues.append(f'not_key_value:{seg}') continue k, v = seg.split('=', 1) if k in kv: issues.append(f'duplicate_key:{k}') kv[k] = v # Check required keys for k in expected_keys: if k not in kv: issues.append(f'missing_key:{k}') # Validate each component if 'zone' in kv and not ZONE_RE.match(f"zone={kv['zone']}"): issues.append('bad_zone') if 'year' in kv and not YEAR_RE.match(f"year={kv['year']}"): issues.append('bad_year') if 'month' in kv and not MONTH_RE.match(f"month={kv['month']}"): issues.append('bad_month') if 'day' in kv and not DAY_RE.match(f"day={kv['day']}"): issues.append('bad_day') if 'eventType' in kv and not EVT_RE.match(f"eventType={kv['eventType']}"): issues.append('bad_eventType') if 'user_bucket' in kv and not BUCKET_RE.match(f"user_bucket={kv['user_bucket']}"): issues.append('bad_user_bucket') # No empty directories if file_count == 0: issues.append('empty_directory') return { 'partition': part, 'ok': len(issues) == 0, 'issues': issues } conformance = [check_partition(p, part_counts[p]) for p in candidate] # Build path plan (first 12 paths) path_plan = paths[:12] # Guidance guidance = ( "Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 supports a hierarchical namespace (HNS), which provides true " "directory semantics (rename/move at folder level, atomic operations, and ACLs on folders). " "Designing predictable layouts like /adls/events/zone=raw/year=YYYY/month=MM/day=DD/eventType=.../user_bucket=NN/ " "simplifies cost and access: lifecycle rules can target stable prefixes (e.g., month or day folders) to tier hot/warm/cold data, " "ACLs can be applied at logical boundaries (dataset/zone/eventType), and query engines can prune partitions efficiently, reducing " "both listing overhead and scan costs." ) out = { 'pathPlan': path_plan, 'lifecycle': lifecycle, 'conformance': conformance, 'guidance': guidance, } print(json.dumps(out, indent=2)) PY"} [code-result] supports a hierarchical namespace (HNS), which provides true directory semantics (rename/move at folder level, atomic operations, and ACLs on folders). Designing predictable layouts like /adls/events/zone=raw/year=YYYY/month=MM/day=DD/eventType=.../user_bucket=NN/ simplifies cost and access: lifecycle rules can target stable prefixes (e.g., month or day folders) to tier hot/warm/cold data, ACLs can be applied at logical boundaries (dataset/zone/eventType), and query engines can prune partitions efficiently, reducing both listing overhead and scan costs." }

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: I'll implement an in-memory facade as a small Python script that constructs: (a) a logical base config with non-secrets and kv:// placeholders, plus a production overlay; (b) a resolver that deep-merges base+overlay and keeps kv:// placeholders intact; (c) a rotation planner that outputs a dual-read rollout with a cutover marker for payments/PRIMARY_KEY v1→v2; and (d) a linter that flags likely embedded secrets. I’ll write the script to /workspace, then run it to produce the requested JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/kv_facade.py << 'PY' import json, re, sys, copy from typing import Any, Dict, List, Tuple # ------------------------------ # In-memory logical configuration # ------------------------------ base_config: Dict[str, Any] = { "app": { "name": "ordersvc", "retries": {"maxAttempts": 4, "backoffMs": 200, "jitter": True}, "featureFlags": {"enableIdempotency": True, "useNewRouter": False}, "http": {"port": 8080, "timeoutMs": 5000}, }, "dependencies": { "db": { "driver": "postgres", # Secret placeholder (versioned): "connectionString": "kv://infra/DB_CONN#v1", }, "payments": { "endpoint": "https://api.payments.example", # Secret placeholder (versioned): "primaryKey": "kv://payments/PRIMARY_KEY#v1", }, "cache": { "provider": "redis", # Secret placeholder (versioned): "url": "kv://infra/REDIS_URL#v3", }, "webhooks": { # Secret placeholder (versioned): "stripeSigningSecret": "kv://webhooks/stripe/signing-secret#vA", }, "email": { "smtpUrl": "smtps://smtp.example:465", # Intentionally bad (for linter to flag): appears to embed a secret "apiKey": "stripe_placeholder_token", }, }, "observability": { "logging": {"level": "INFO"}, "metrics": {"enabled": True, "exporter": "otlp", "endpoint": "https://otlp.example:4318"}, }, } # Environment overlay (production) prod_overlay: Dict[str, Any] = { "app": { "http": {"port": 80}, "featureFlags": {"useNewRouter": True}, }, "dependencies": { "payments": {"endpoint": "https://api.pay.payments.example"}, # Demonstrate infra redis URL bumped in prod "cache": {"url": "kv://infra/REDIS_URL#v4"}, }, } def deep_merge(a: Dict[str, Any], b: Dict[str, Any]) -> Dict[str, Any]: out = copy.deepcopy(a) for k, v in b.items(): if k in out and isinstance(out[k], dict) and isinstance(v, dict): out[k] = deep_merge(out[k], v) else: out[k] = copy.deepcopy(v) return out # ------------------------------ # Resolver (keeps placeholders) # ------------------------------ def resolve_config(base: Dict[str, Any], overlay: Dict[str, Any]) -> Dict[str, Any]: # Strictly in-memory deep merge, no secret fetching. merged = deep_merge(base, overlay) # Opaque token concept (not exposed): we could map kv:// URIs -> opaque handles here # but per request, return placeholders intact in resolvedConfig. return merged # ------------------------------ # Rotation planner (dual-read) # ------------------------------ def rotation_plan(secret_path: str, from_version: str, to_version: str) -> List[Dict[str, Any]]: # secret_path example: "payments/PRIMARY_KEY" (Key Vault secret name path) # We will reference placeholders like kv://payments/PRIMARY_KEY#v1 and #v2 v1_uri = f"kv://{secret_path}#{from_version}" v2_uri = f"kv://{secret_path}#{to_version}" steps: List[Dict[str, Any]] = [ { "id": 1, "title": "Prepare dual-read (no traffic shift yet)", "description": ( "Publish config with primary still at v1 and add candidates [v2, v1] for dual-read. " "Clients attempt v2 first with fallback to v1; do not revoke v1." ), "configPatch": { "dependencies.payments.primaryKey": v1_uri, "dependencies.payments.primaryKeyCandidates": [v2_uri, v1_uri], "flags.cutoverReady": False, }, "holdDuration": "PT1H", "checks": [ "v2 exists in Key Vault and is accessible", "error rate unchanged in shadow/dual-read", ], }, { "id": 2, "title": "Canary: small percentage with dual-read", "description": ( "Enable dual-read for 5-10% of traffic using candidates [v2, v1]. " "Collect latency, error, and auth failure metrics; keep caches short (<=5m)." ), "configPatch": { "trafficPolicy": {"dualReadPercent": 0.1}, "dependencies.payments.primaryKeyCandidates": [v2_uri, v1_uri], }, "holdDuration": "PT2H", "checks": [ "p50/p95 latency within SLO", "auth failures not elevated", "no increase in 4xx/5xx compared to baseline", ], }, { "id": 3, "title": "Dual-read full: mark cutover-ready", "description": ( "Roll dual-read to 100% of traffic. If stable for a full error budget window, " "set a cutover marker to proceed." ), "configPatch": { "trafficPolicy": {"dualReadPercent": 1.0}, "dependencies.payments.primaryKeyCandidates": [v2_uri, v1_uri], "flags.cutoverReady": True, }, "holdDuration": "PT24H", "checks": [ "error budget not consumed", "no auth regressions for v2", ], }, { "id": 4, "title": "Cutover: switch primary to v2, keep fallback", "description": ( "Flip the primaryKey to v2. Keep candidates [v2, v1] for 24-48h as safety net; " "do not revoke v1 yet." ), "configPatch": { "dependencies.payments.primaryKey": v2_uri, "dependencies.payments.primaryKeyCandidates": [v2_uri, v1_uri], "flags.cutoverReady": True, }, "holdDuration": "PT24H", "checks": [ "no step-change in errors post-cutover", "credential usage shows v2 predominates", ], }, { "id": 5, "title": "Cleanup: remove fallback and deprecate v1", "description": ( "Remove v1 from candidate list, clear caches, then disable/expire v1 in Key Vault." ), "configPatch": { "dependencies.payments.primaryKey": v2_uri, "dependencies.payments.primaryKeyCandidates": [v2_uri], "flags.cutoverReady": True, }, "holdDuration": "PT0S", "checks": [ "no remaining callers require v1", "no auth failures after v1 deprecation", ], }, ] return steps # ------------------------------ # Linter (flag embedded secrets) # ------------------------------ SUSPECT_KEYS = [ "secret", "passwd", "password", "token", "apikey", "api_key", "key", "privatekey", "private_key", "pem", "bearer", "connectionstring", "dsn", "conn", ] base64ish = re.compile(r"^[A-Za-z0-9+/=_-]{24,}$") jwt_rx = re.compile(r"^[A-Za-z0-9-_]+\.[A-Za-z0-9-_]+\.[A-Za-z0-9-_]+$") uri_with_creds = re.compile(r"^[a-z]+://[^/@:]+:[^/@:]+@[^\s]+$") looks_hex = re.compile(r"^[0-9a-fA-F]{32,}$") def mafake_key_value(v: str) -> str: if len(v) <= 8: return "***" return v[:4] + "…" + v[-4:] def linter(config: Dict[str, Any]) -> List[Dict[str, Any]]: findings: List[Dict[str, Any]] = [] def walk(obj: Any, path: List[str]): if isinstance(obj, dict): for k, v in obj.items(): walk(v, path + [str(k)]) elif isinstance(obj, list): for i, v in enumerate(obj): walk(v, path + [str(i)]) else: # leaf if isinstance(obj, str): key_lower = path[-1].lower() if path else "" val = obj is_kv_placeholder = val.startswith("kv://") # Flag suspicious values that are NOT kv placeholders if not is_kv_placeholder: reasons: List[str] = [] if any(tok in key_lower for tok in SUSPECT_KEYS): reasons.append("sensitive-looking key name") if val.startswith("fake_key_") or val.startswith("ghp_") or val.startswith("AKIA"): reasons.append("value resembles cloud/API key prefix") if "-----BEGIN " in val: reasons.append("value resembles PEM/PK material") if jwt_rx.match(val): reasons.append("value resembles JWT token format") if base64ish.match(val) and not val.startswith("http"): reasons.append("value resembles base64/opaque secret") if looks_hex.match(val): reasons.append("value resembles long hex secret") if uri_with_creds.match(val): reasons.append("URI contains inline credentials") if reasons: findings.append({ "path": ".".join(path), "key": path[-1] if path else "", "valuePreview": mafake_key_value(val), "severity": "error" if ("secret" in key_lower or "password" in key_lower) else "warning", "reason": ", ".join(sorted(set(reasons))), "remediation": "Replace with Key Vault reference (kv:///# or versionless).", }) walk(config, []) return findings # ------------------------------ # Compose output # ------------------------------ resolved = resolve_config(base_config, prod_overlay) plan = rotation_plan(secret_path="payments/PRIMARY_KEY", from_version="v1", to_version="v2") issues = linter(resolved) notes = ( "Using indirection (kv:// placeholders) avoids embedding secrets in config and lets services resolve credentials at runtime while rotation windows with dual-read fallbacks enable safe cutovers without breaking traffic. " "In Azure Key Vault, secrets have DNS-style URIs where the name (letters, digits, hyphens) identifies the secret and each update creates a new immutable version (a GUID); clients may reference a versionless name for automatic latest or a version-pinned identifier for controlled rollouts." ) out = { "resolvedConfig": resolved, "rotationPlan": plan, "lintFindings": issues, "notes": notes, } print(json.dumps(out, indent=2)) PY python3 /workspace/kv_facade.py"}

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: I will implement an in-memory microservice contract with Python: define handlers for GET /health, GET /ready, and POST /predict, add strict input validation, standardized error responses, and a latency measurement. Then I’ll write a contract tester that exercises valid and invalid requests, summarizes pass/fail, and prints the requested JSON bundle. I’ll start by scaffolding the project and writing service.py. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/aca_microservice && cat > /workspace/aca_microservice/service.py << 'PY' from __future__ import annotations import time import math from typing import Any, Dict, List, Tuple, Optional # Framework-agnostic, zero-I/O microservice primitives # Endpoints to simulate: # - GET /health -> {"status", "version"} # - GET /ready -> readiness probe simulated -> {"ready", "version"} # - POST /predict -> accepts typed features -> returns {"proba","label","modelVersion","latencyMs"} # Input validation: required fields, types, ranges; reject unknown fields. # Error responses: consistent shape for errors across endpoints that can error out. MODEL_VERSION = "1.2.3" # Define feature contract FEATURE_SPEC: Dict[str, Dict[str, Any]] = { "age": {"type": "int", "required": True, "min": 0, "max": 120}, "income": {"type": "number", "required": True, "min": 0.0, "max": 1_000_000.0}, "isMember": {"type": "bool", "required": True}, "country": { "type": "string", "required": True, "enum": ["US", "CA", "GB", "DE", "FR", "IN", "JP", "AU"], }, } # Error builder: consistent shape # For predict errors we include modelVersion; for others we may omit modelVersion if not relevant. def build_error(code: str, message: str, status: int, details: Optional[Dict[str, Any]] = None, *, model_version: Optional[str] = None, latency_ms: Optional[float] = None) -> Dict[str, Any]: err: Dict[str, Any] = { "error": { "code": code, "message": message, }, "status": status, } if details is not None: err["error"]["details"] = details if model_version is not None: err["modelVersion"] = model_version if latency_ms is not None: err["latencyMs"] = round(float(latency_ms), 3) return err # Simple scoring function (logistic) # proba = sigmoid(w0 + w_age*age + w_income*income + w_member*isMember + w_country[country]) # Scales chosen to produce reasonable probabilities within defined ranges. WEIGHTS = { "w0": -2.5, "age": 0.02, # +2% to log-odds per year "income": 0.000004, # +0.000004 per $1 income (~+4 to log-odds at $1M) "isMember": 0.6, # membership benefit } COUNTRY_BIAS = { "US": 0.2, "CA": 0.15, "GB": 0.1, "DE": 0.05, "FR": 0.05, "IN": -0.05, "JP": 0.0, "AU": 0.1, } def _sigmoid(x: float) -> float: # numerical stability if x >= 0: z = math.exp(-x) return 1.0 / (1.0 + z) else: z = math.exp(x) return z / (1.0 + z) class LatencySampler: def __init__(self) -> None: self.count = 0 self.ewma_ms = 0.0 self.last_ms = 0.0 def observe(self, ms: float) -> None: self.last_ms = ms self.count += 1 alpha = 0.2 # EWMA smoothing factor if self.count == 1: self.ewma_ms = ms else: self.ewma_ms = alpha * ms + (1 - alpha) * self.ewma_ms def snapshot(self) -> Dict[str, Any]: return { "requests": self.count, "ewmaMs": round(self.ewma_ms, 3), "lastMs": round(self.last_ms, 3), } class Service: def __init__(self, version: str = MODEL_VERSION): self.version = version self._ready = False # simulate warm-up readiness self.latency = LatencySampler() # Ops controls (internal, not HTTP): def set_ready(self, ready: bool) -> None: self._ready = bool(ready) # Endpoint: GET /health def get_health(self) -> Tuple[Dict[str, Any], int]: # Liveness probe: process is up, no deep checks here. return {"status": "ok", "version": self.version}, 200 # Endpoint: GET /ready def get_ready(self) -> Tuple[Dict[str, Any], int]: # Readiness probe: can this instance accept traffic? return {"ready": bool(self._ready), "version": self.version}, 200 # Endpoint: POST /predict def post_predict(self, body: Any) -> Tuple[Dict[str, Any], int]: t0 = time.perf_counter() # Validate request body type if not isinstance(body, dict): t1 = time.perf_counter() ms = (t1 - t0) * 1000.0 return build_error( code="BAD_REQUEST", message="Request body must be a JSON object", status=400, details={"expected": "object", "actual": type(body).__name__}, model_version=self.version, latency_ms=ms, ), 400 errors: List[Dict[str, Any]] = [] # Required checks for fname, spec in FEATURE_SPEC.items(): if spec.get("required") and fname not in body: errors.append({ "field": fname, "issue": "missing", "expected": "required", "actual": None, }) # Unknown field checks for key in body.keys(): if key not in FEATURE_SPEC: errors.append({ "field": key, "issue": "unknown", "expected": "not allowed", "actual": body.get(key), }) # Type and range checks features: Dict[str, Any] = {} for fname, spec in FEATURE_SPEC.items(): if fname not in body: continue # already captured by required check val = body[fname] ftype = spec["type"] if ftype == "int": if not isinstance(val, int): errors.append({ "field": fname, "issue": "type", "expected": "int", "actual": type(val).__name__, }) else: # range minv = spec.get("min") maxv = spec.get("max") if minv is not None and val < minv: errors.append({ "field": fname, "issue": "range", "expected": f">= {minv}", "actual": val, }) if maxv is not None and val > maxv: errors.append({ "field": fname, "issue": "range", "expected": f"<= {maxv}", "actual": val, }) features[fname] = val elif ftype == "number": if not isinstance(val, (int, float)) or isinstance(val, bool): # note: bool is a subclass of int; exclude bool errors.append({ "field": fname, "issue": "type", "expected": "number", "actual": type(val).__name__, }) else: fval = float(val) minv = spec.get("min") maxv = spec.get("max") if minv is not None and fval < float(minv): errors.append({ "field": fname, "issue": "range", "expected": f">= {minv}", "actual": fval, }) if maxv is not None and fval > float(maxv): errors.append({ "field": fname, "issue": "range", "expected": f"<= {maxv}", "actual": fval, }) features[fname] = fval elif ftype == "bool": if not isinstance(val, bool): errors.append({ "field": fname, "issue": "type", "expected": "bool", "actual": type(val).__name__, }) else: features[fname] = bool(val) elif ftype == "string": if not isinstance(val, str): errors.append({ "field": fname, "issue": "type", "expected": "string", "actual": type(val).__name__, }) else: enum = spec.get("enum") if enum is not None and val not in enum: errors.append({ "field": fname, "issue": "enum", "expected": enum, "actual": val, }) features[fname] = val else: errors.append({ "field": fname, "issue": "spec", "expected": f"unknown type in spec: {ftype}", "actual": None, }) if errors: t1 = time.perf_counter() ms = (t1 - t0) * 1000.0 # All validation issues -> 400 resp = build_error( code="VALIDATION_ERROR", message="One or more validation errors occurred", status=400, details={"fieldErrors": errors}, model_version=self.version, latency_ms=ms, ) self.latency.observe(ms) return resp, 400 # Scoring logit = ( WEIGHTS["w0"] + WEIGHTS["age"] * float(features["age"]) + WEIGHTS["income"] * float(features["income"]) + (WEIGHTS["isMember"] if features["isMember"] else 0.0) + COUNTRY_BIAS.get(features["country"], 0.0) ) proba = _sigmoid(logit) label = "approve" if proba >= 0.5 else "deny" t1 = time.perf_counter() ms = (t1 - t0) * 1000.0 self.latency.observe(ms) return { "proba": round(proba, 6), "label": label, "modelVersion": self.version, "latencyMs": round(ms, 3), }, 200 # Minimal OpenAPI-like sketch generator (framework-agnostic) def openapi_sketch(version: str = MODEL_VERSION) -> Dict[str, Any]: return { "openapi": "3.0.0", "info": {"title": "Prediction Microservice", "version": version}, "paths": { "/health": { "get": { "summary": "Liveness probe", "responses": { "200": { "description": "Service is alive", "content": { "application/json": { "schema": { "type": "object", "required": ["status", "version"], "properties": { "status": {"type": "string", "enum": ["ok"]}, "version": {"type": "string"}, }, } } }, } }, } }, "/ready": { "get": { "summary": "Readiness probe", "responses": { "200": { "description": "Readiness state", "content": { "application/json": { "schema": { "type": "object", "required": ["ready", "version"], "properties": { "ready": {"type": "boolean"}, "version": {"type": "string"}, }, } } }, } }, } }, "/predict": { "post": { "summary": "Score a request", "requestBody": { "required": True, "content": { "application/json": { "schema": { "type": "object", "additionalProperties": False, "required": [k for k, v in FEATURE_SPEC.items() if v.get("required")], "properties": { "age": {"type": "integer", "minimum": 0, "maximum": 120}, "income": {"type": "number", "minimum": 0, "maximum": 1000000}, "isMember": {"type": "boolean"}, "country": {"type": "string", "enum": FEATURE_SPEC["country"]["enum"]}, }, } } }, }, "responses": { "200": { "description": "Score produced", "content": { "application/json": { "schema": { "type": "object", "required": ["proba", "label", "modelVersion", "latencyMs"], "properties": { "proba": {"type": "number", "minimum": 0, "maximum": 1}, "label": {"type": "string", "enum": ["approve", "deny"]}, "modelVersion": {"type": "string"}, "latencyMs": {"type": "number", "minimum": 0}, }, } } }, }, "400": { "description": "Validation error", "content": { "application/json": { "schema": { "type": "object", "required": ["error", "status", "modelVersion"], "properties": { "error": { "type": "object", "required": ["code", "message"], "properties": { "code": {"type": "string"}, "message": {"type": "string"}, "details": {"type": "object"}, }, }, "status": {"type": "integer"}, "modelVersion": {"type": "string"}, "latencyMs": {"type": "number"}, }, } } }, }, }, } }, }, } PY"}

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results.
