Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: Plan: Implement a small, in-memory Python data-quality engine with a simple rule DSL, a schema validator, profiling, and a gate decision. Then generate deterministic synthetic rows with deliberate issues, run the engine, and print the structured result JSON. Steps: 1) Scaffold project in /workspace/dq_engine. 2) Implement engine (schema checks, rule evaluation with severity, profile). 3) Implement deterministic synthetic data generator. 4) Run the engine and output the requested JSON structure. 5) Verify output. {"type":"code","code_command":"set -e mkdir -p /workspace/dq_engine cat > /workspace/dq_engine/dq_engine.py << 'PY' from __future__ import annotations from dataclasses import dataclass from typing import Any, Dict, List, Optional, Tuple import json import math @dataclass class Rule: id: str col: Optional[str] op: str # one of: '>=', '>', '<=', '<', '==', '!=', 'in', 'not_null', 'regex', 'between', 'length<=', 'length>=', 'custom' value: Any = None severity: str = "ERROR" # 'ERROR' or 'WARN' description: str = "" def to_dict(self) -> Dict[str, Any]: return { "id": self.id, "col": self.col, "op": self.op, "value": self.value, "severity": self.severity, "description": self.description, } class DataQualityEngine: def __init__(self, sample_limit: int = 5): self.sample_limit = sample_limit @staticmethod def _is_null(v: Any) -> bool: return v is None or (isinstance(v, float) and math.isnan(v)) def _check_rule(self, row: Dict[str, Any], rule: Rule) -> bool: # Returns True if row passes the rule. v = row.get(rule.col) if rule.col else None try: if rule.op == 'not_null': return not self._is_null(v) if rule.op == 'in': return v in rule.value if rule.op == '==': return v == rule.value if rule.op == '!=': return v != rule.value if rule.op == '>=': return v >= rule.value if rule.op == '>': return v > rule.value if rule.op == '<=': return v <= rule.value if rule.op == '<': return v < rule.value if rule.op == 'between': lo, hi = rule.value return (v is not None) and (lo <= v <= hi) if rule.op == 'length<=': return (v is None) or (len(str(v)) <= int(rule.value)) if rule.op == 'length>=': return (v is not None) and (len(str(v)) >= int(rule.value)) if rule.op == 'custom': # value should be a callable(row) -> bool return bool(rule.value(row)) except Exception: # Any exception means rule not satisfied (e.g., type mismatch) return False # Unknown op → fail safe return False def _build_schema_rules(self, schema: Dict[str, Dict[str, Any]]) -> List[Rule]: schema_rules: List[Rule] = [] for col, spec in schema.items(): # Required → not_null if spec.get('required', False): schema_rules.append(Rule( id=f"schema:{col}:not_null", col=col, op='not_null', severity='ERROR', description=f"{col} must not be null (required)" )) # Type check rule expected_type = spec.get('type') if expected_type: def make_type_checker(colname: str, tname: str): def _check(row: Dict[str, Any]) -> bool: v = row.get(colname) if v is None: return True # nullability handled by not_null if required if tname == 'int': return isinstance(v, int) and not isinstance(v, bool) if tname == 'float': return (isinstance(v, (int, float)) and not isinstance(v, bool)) if tname == 'str': return isinstance(v, str) if tname == 'bool': return isinstance(v, bool) # default: accept any return True return _check schema_rules.append(Rule( id=f"schema:{col}:type_{expected_type}", col=None, op='custom', value=make_type_checker(col, expected_type), severity='ERROR', description=f"{col} must be of type {expected_type}" )) # Uniqueness constraints as schema-level rule for col, spec in schema.items(): if spec.get('unique', False): def make_unique_checker(colname: str): seen = set() def _checker_stateful(row: Dict[str, Any]) -> bool: v = row.get(colname) # None values considered non-unique failure if required, else treat unique on non-null key = v if key in seen: return False seen.add(key) return True return _checker_stateful schema_rules.append(Rule( id=f"schema:{col}:unique", col=None, op='custom', value=make_unique_checker(col), severity='ERROR', description=f"{col} must be unique" )) return schema_rules def profile(self, rows: List[Dict[str, Any]], schema: Dict[str, Dict[str, Any]]) -> Dict[str, Any]: profile: Dict[str, Any] = {} n = len(rows) for col in schema.keys(): values = [r.get(col) for r in rows] non_null_vals = [v for v in values if v is not None and not (isinstance(v, float) and math.isnan(v))] completeness = (len(non_null_vals) / n) if n else 0.0 unique_non_null = len(set(non_null_vals)) if non_null_vals else 0 uniqueness = (unique_non_null / len(non_null_vals)) if non_null_vals else 0.0 col_profile: Dict[str, Any] = { 'count': n, 'nonNullCount': len(non_null_vals), 'completeness': round(completeness, 6), 'uniqueness': round(uniqueness, 6), } # numeric stats if applicable numeric_vals = [float(v) for v in non_null_vals if isinstance(v, (int, float)) and not isinstance(v, bool)] if numeric_vals: col_profile.update({ 'min': min(numeric_vals), 'max': max(numeric_vals), 'mean': sum(numeric_vals)/len(numeric_vals) }) profile[col] = col_profile return profile def evaluate(self, rows: List[Dict[str, Any]], schema: Dict[str, Dict[str, Any]], rules: List[Rule]) -> Dict[str, Any]: # Build schema-derived rules (required, type, unique) all_rules = self._build_schema_rules(schema) + rules # Initialize counters and samples rule_stats: Dict[str, Dict[str, Any]] = {} violations: Dict[str, List[Dict[str, Any]]] = {} for rule in all_rules: rule_stats[rule.id] = { 'id': rule.id, 'description': rule.description, 'severity': rule.severity, 'total': len(rows), 'pass': 0, 'fail': 0, } violations[rule.id] = [] # Evaluate for idx, row in enumerate(rows): for rule in all_rules: ok = self._check_rule(row, rule) if ok: rule_stats[rule.id]['pass'] += 1 else: rule_stats[rule.id]['fail'] += 1 if len(violations[rule.id]) < self.sample_limit: sample = {"rowIndex": idx, **row} violations[rule.id].append(sample) # Profile prof = self.profile(rows, schema) # Gate decision decision = 'PASS' reasons: List[str] = [] for rid, st in rule_stats.items(): if st['fail'] > 0 and st['severity'] == 'ERROR': decision = 'FAIL' reasons.append(f"{rid} failed {st['fail']}/{st['total']}") gate = { 'decision': decision, 'reasons': reasons } rulesEvaluated = list(rule_stats.values()) return { 'rulesEvaluated': rulesEvaluated, 'violations': violations, 'profile': prof, 'gate': gate } # Convenience to run from another script def run_engine(rows: List[Dict[str, Any]], schema: Dict[str, Dict[str, Any]], rule_dicts: List[Dict[str, Any]], sample_limit: int = 5) -> Dict[str, Any]: rules = [Rule(**rd) for rd in rule_dicts] eng = DataQualityEngine(sample_limit=sample_limit) return eng.evaluate(rows, schema, rules) PY cat > /workspace/dq_engine/generate_data.py << 'PY' from typing import List, Dict, Any def synthetic_rows() -> List[Dict[str, Any]]: # Deterministic, hand-authored rows with issues # Schema: id:int (required, unique), amount:float (required), status:str (required), ts:str (required) rows = [ {"id": 1, "amount": 100.0, "status": "NEW", "ts": "2025-01-01T10:00:00Z"}, {"id": 2, "amount": -5.0, "status": "PAID", "ts": "2025-01-02T10:00:00Z"}, # negative amount {"id": 3, "amount": 0.0, "status": "VOID", "ts": "2025-01-03T10:00:00Z"}, {"id": 4, "amount": 15000.0,"status": "PAID", "ts": "2025-01-04T10:00:00Z"}, # high amount (WARN) {"id": 5, "amount": 55.5, "status": "PENDING","ts": "2025-01-05T10:00:00Z"}, # invalid status {"id": 6, "amount": None, "status": "NEW", "ts": "2025-01-06T10:00:00Z"}, # null amount {"id": 7, "amount": 10.0, "status": "NEW", "ts": None}, # null ts {"id": 8, "amount": 80.0, "status": "PAID", "ts": "2025-01-08T10:00:00Z"}, {"id": 8, "amount": 80.0, "status": "PAID", "ts": "2025-01-08T10:00:00Z"}, # duplicate id {"id": 9, "amount": "100", "status": "VOID", "ts": "2025-01-09T10:00:00Z"}, # amount wrong type {"id": 10, "amount": 9999.9, "status": "NEW", "ts": "2025-01-10T10:00:00Z"}, {"id": 11, "amount": 0.01, "status": "PAID", "ts": "2025-01-11T10:00:00Z"}, ] return rows PY cat > /workspace/dq_engine/run_engine.py << 'PY' import json from dq_engine import run_engine from generate_data import synthetic_rows schema = { 'id': {'type': 'int', 'required': True, 'unique': True}, 'amount': {'type': 'float', 'required': True}, 'status': {'type': 'str', 'required': True}, 'ts': {'type': 'str', 'required': True}, } rules = [ { 'id': 'amount_nonnegative', 'col': 'amount', 'op': '>=', 'value': 0, 'severity': 'ERROR', 'description': 'amount must be >= 0' }, { 'id': 'status_allowed', 'col': 'status', 'op': 'in', 'value': ['NEW','PAID','VOID'], 'severity': 'ERROR', 'description': "status must be one of {'NEW','PAID','VOID'}" }, { 'id': 'ts_not_null_rule', # explicit rule in addition to schema 'col': 'ts', 'op': 'not_null', 'value': None, 'severity': 'ERROR', 'description': 'ts must not be null' }, { 'id': 'amount_reasonable_warn', 'col': 'amount', 'op': '<=', 'value': 10000.0, 'severity': 'WARN', 'description': 'amount should be <= 10000 (warn)' }, ] rows = synthetic_rows() result = run_engine(rows, schema, rules, sample_limit=5) print(json.dumps(result, indent=2, sort_keys=False)) # Save to file for inspection with open('/workspace/dq_engine/output.json', 'w') as f: json.dump(result, f, indent=2) PY python3 /workspace/dq_engine/run_engine.py"}

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: I'll implement the in-memory EDA routine as a single Python script that generates seeded synthetic reference/current datasets, computes per-feature summaries, applies KS (numeric) and PSI (categorical), derives slice examples at the most pronounced drift points, and prints the required JSON payload. I'll run it via a code tool and then return the JSON and a brief rationale. {"type":"code","code_command":"python - << 'PY' import json, random, math from collections import Counter, defaultdict from statistics import mean, pstdev from bisect import bisect_right # Seed all randomness SEED = 42 random.seed(SEED) # Small synthetic datasets (all in-memory) N_REF = 400 N_CUR = 400 # Feature schema: numeric and categorical num_features = ["num_stable", "num_shift_mean", "num_shift_var"] cat_features = ["cat_stable", "cat_shift"] # Distributions # Numeric # ref: num_stable ~ N(0,1); cur: same # ref: num_shift_mean ~ N(0,1); cur: N(0.5,1) # ref: num_shift_var ~ N(0,1); cur: N(0,1.4) # Categorical # ref: cat_stable ~ {X:0.5,Y:0.5}; cur: same # ref: cat_shift ~ {A:0.6,B:0.3,C:0.1}; cur: {A:0.45,B:0.35,C:0.20} ref_rows = [] cur_rows = [] # Helpers to sample categorical def sample_cat(dist): keys = list(dist.keys()) weights = [dist[k] for k in keys] # normalize in case of float rounding s = sum(weights) if s <= 0: weights = [1.0/len(keys)]*len(keys) else: weights = [w/s for w in weights] r = random.random() acc = 0.0 for k, w in zip(keys, weights): acc += w if r <= acc: return k return keys[-1] # Generate reference for i in range(N_REF): row = {"id": f"ref_{i}"} # Numeric row["num_stable"] = random.gauss(0.0, 1.0) row["num_shift_mean"] = random.gauss(0.0, 1.0) row["num_shift_var"] = random.gauss(0.0, 1.0) # Categorical row["cat_stable"] = sample_cat({"X":0.5, "Y":0.5}) row["cat_shift"] = sample_cat({"A":0.6, "B":0.3, "C":0.1}) ref_rows.append(row) # Generate current for i in range(N_CUR): row = {"id": f"cur_{i}"} row["num_stable"] = random.gauss(0.0, 1.0) row["num_shift_mean"] = random.gauss(0.5, 1.0) row["num_shift_var"] = random.gauss(0.0, 1.4) row["cat_stable"] = sample_cat({"X":0.5, "Y":0.5}) row["cat_shift"] = sample_cat({"A":0.45, "B":0.35, "C":0.20}) cur_rows.append(row) # Extract per-feature columns + id indexes for slice examples ref_cols = {f: [r[f] for r in ref_rows] for f in num_features + cat_features} cur_cols = {f: [r[f] for r in cur_rows] for f in num_features + cat_features} ref_ids_by_feature_value = {f: [(r["id"], r[f]) for r in ref_rows] for f in num_features + cat_features} cur_ids_by_feature_value = {f: [(r["id"], r[f]) for r in cur_rows] for f in num_features + cat_features} # Summary calculations def numeric_summary(vals): return { "mean": float(mean(vals)) if vals else None, "std": float(pstdev(vals)) if len(vals) > 1 else 0.0, "min": float(min(vals)) if vals else None, "max": float(max(vals)) if vals else None, } def cat_summary(vals): c = Counter(vals) total = sum(c.values()) props = {k: v/total for k, v in c.items()} if total else {} return { "counts": dict(c), "proportions": props } summaryRef = {"numeric": {}, "categorical": {}} summaryCur = {"numeric": {}, "categorical": {}} for f in num_features: summaryRef["numeric"][f] = numeric_summary(ref_cols[f]) summaryCur["numeric"][f] = numeric_summary(cur_cols[f]) for f in cat_features: summaryRef["categorical"][f] = cat_summary(ref_cols[f]) summaryCur["categorical"][f] = cat_summary(cur_cols[f]) # Drift metrics # KS for numeric def ks_stat(x, y): xs = sorted(x) ys = sorted(y) nx = len(xs) ny = len(ys) all_vals = sorted(set(xs + ys)) i = j = 0 D = 0.0 arg_t = None arg_diff = 0.0 # We'll compute CDF at each unique value using bisect for clarity for v in all_vals: cx = bisect_right(xs, v) cy = bisect_right(ys, v) Fx = cx / nx Fy = cy / ny diff = Fy - Fx ad = abs(diff) if ad > D: D = ad arg_t = v arg_diff = diff return D, arg_t, arg_diff # PSI for categorical def psi_categorical(ref_vals, cur_vals, eps=1e-6): cref = Counter(ref_vals) ccur = Counter(cur_vals) cats = set(cref.keys()) | set(ccur.keys()) nref = sum(cref.values()) ncur = sum(ccur.values()) psi = 0.0 deltas = {} for cat in sorted(cats): p_ref = (cref.get(cat, 0) / nref) if nref else 0.0 p_cur = (ccur.get(cat, 0) / ncur) if ncur else 0.0 # smooth to avoid div by zero/log(0) p_ref_s = max(p_ref, eps) p_cur_s = max(p_cur, eps) psi += (p_cur_s - p_ref_s) * math.log(p_cur_s / p_ref_s) deltas[cat] = p_cur - p_ref return psi, deltas # Thresholds KS_WARN = 0.10 KS_ALERT = 0.20 PSI_WARN = 0.10 PSI_ALERT = 0.25 # Drift findings + slices driftFindings = [] slices = [] # Numeric drift numeric_scores = {} for f in num_features: score, t_star, diff_at_t = ks_stat(ref_cols[f], cur_cols[f]) flag = "ok" if score >= KS_ALERT: flag = "alert" elif score >= KS_WARN: flag = "warn" driftFindings.append({ "feature": f, "type": "numeric", "test": "KS", "score": round(float(score), 6), "flag": flag }) numeric_scores[f] = score # Build slice example at t_star if t_star is not None: # If diff > 0 => cur CDF greater => cur has more mass <= t_star # Slice is x <= t_star if diff>0 else x > t_star cond_le = diff_at_t > 0 ref_vals = ref_ids_by_feature_value[f] cur_vals = cur_ids_by_feature_value[f] if cond_le: ref_slice_ids = [rid for rid, val in ref_vals if isinstance(val, (int, float)) and val <= t_star] cur_slice_ids = [rid for rid, val in cur_vals if isinstance(val, (int, float)) and val <= t_star] cond_str = f"{f} <= {round(t_star, 4)}" else: ref_slice_ids = [rid for rid, val in ref_vals if isinstance(val, (int, float)) and val > t_star] cur_slice_ids = [rid for rid, val in cur_vals if isinstance(val, (int, float)) and val > t_star] cond_str = f"{f} > {round(t_star, 4)}" ref_rate = len(ref_slice_ids) / len(ref_vals) if ref_vals else 0.0 cur_rate = len(cur_slice_ids) / len(cur_vals) if cur_vals else 0.0 slices.append({ "feature": f, "type": "numeric", "condition": cond_str, "ref_rate": round(ref_rate, 6), "cur_rate": round(cur_rate, 6), "delta": round(cur_rate - ref_rate, 6), "ref_examples": ref_slice_ids[:5], "cur_examples": cur_slice_ids[:5] }) # Categorical drift categorical_scores = {} for f in cat_features: psi, deltas = psi_categorical(ref_cols[f], cur_cols[f]) flag = "ok" if psi >= PSI_ALERT: flag = "alert" elif psi >= PSI_WARN: flag = "warn" driftFindings.append({ "feature": f, "type": "categorical", "test": "PSI", "score": round(float(psi), 6), "flag": flag }) categorical_scores[f] = psi # Slice example: category with largest absolute delta if deltas: cat_star = max(deltas.keys(), key=lambda k: abs(deltas[k])) ref_ids = [rid for rid, val in ref_ids_by_feature_value[f] if val == cat_star] cur_ids = [rid for rid, val in cur_ids_by_feature_value[f] if val == cat_star] ref_rate = len(ref_ids) / len(ref_rows) if ref_rows else 0.0 cur_rate = len(cur_ids) / len(cur_rows) if cur_rows else 0.0 slices.append({ "feature": f, "type": "categorical", "category": cat_star, "condition": f"{f} == '{cat_star}'", "ref_rate": round(ref_rate, 6), "cur_rate": round(cur_rate, 6), "delta": round(cur_rate - ref_rate, 6), "ref_examples": ref_ids[:5], "cur_examples": cur_ids[:5] }) # Watchlist: top 3 features across numeric (KS) and categorical (PSI) # Normalize scores by alert thresholds to compare apples-to-apples watch_candidates = [] for f, s in numeric_scores.items(): watch_candidates.append({ "feature": f, "type": "numeric", "test": "KS", "score": float(s), "warnThreshold": KS_WARN, "alertThreshold": KS_ALERT, "normalized": float(s/KS_ALERT) if KS_ALERT > 0 else float(s) }) for f, s in categorical_scores.items(): watch_candidates.append({ "feature": f, "type": "categorical", "test": "PSI", "score": float(s), "warnThreshold": PSI_WARN, "alertThreshold": PSI_ALERT, "normalized": float(s/PSI_ALERT) if PSI_ALERT > 0 else float(s) }) watch_candidates.sort(key=lambda d: d["normalized"], reverse=True) watchlist = [] for w in watch_candidates[:3]: watchlist.append({ "feature": w["feature"], "type": w["type"], "test": w["test"], "score": round(w["score"], 6), "suggestedWarnThreshold": w["warnThreshold"], "suggestedAlertThreshold": w["alertThreshold"] }) rationale = ( "Thresholds chosen for early detection vs. escalation: KS>0.10 and PSI>0.10 as early warnings, " "KS>0.20 and PSI>0.25 as alerts. KS captures any distributional change in numeric features; PSI flags " "category proportion shifts. These thresholds are commonly used heuristics to balance sensitivity and false alarms. " "Connect to alerting by logging scores to metrics and emitting an event (e.g., webhook/message) whenever any 'alert' flag is observed, " "and creating a watchlist to increase sampling/validation frequency for features near the warn thresholds. Randomness is seeded for reproducibility." ) result = { "summaryRef": summaryRef, "summaryCur": summaryCur, "driftFindings": driftFindings, "watchlist": watchlist, "sliceExamples": slices, # slice examples where drift is most pronounced "rationale": rationale } print(json.dumps(result, indent=2)) PY"}

MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: I'll implement an in-memory Python script that generates synthetic transactions, defines a feature registry, and computes point-in-time safe features with a retrieval function. Then I’ll run it to produce the final JSON with registry, exampleVectors, freshnessReport, and notes. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/mini_feature_store.py << 'PY' import random, time, json from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone from collections import defaultdict # ----------------------------- # Synthetic data generation # ----------------------------- def epoch(dt: datetime) -> int: return int(dt.replace(tzinfo=timezone.utc).timestamp()) def to_iso(ts: int) -> str: return datetime.fromtimestamp(ts, tz=timezone.utc).isoformat() random.seed(42) # Choose a fixed cutoff for demonstration cutoff_dt = datetime(2025, 6, 1, 0, 0, 0, tzinfo=timezone.utc) cutoff_ts = epoch(cutoff_dt) start_dt = cutoff_dt - timedelta(days=30) start_ts = epoch(start_dt) end_future_dt = cutoff_dt + timedelta(days=3) # include some future events to test PIT correctness end_future_ts = epoch(end_future_dt) channels = ["web", "mobile", "pos", "ivr"] # Create users num_users = 20 users = [f"u{uid:04d}" for uid in range(1, num_users + 1)] transactions = [] transactions_by_user = defaultdict(list) # Generate synthetic transactions (with some after-cutoff to prove PIT safety) for u in users: n = random.randint(15, 80) # variable activity per user for _ in range(n): ts = random.randint(start_ts, end_future_ts) # may include future relative to cutoff # lognormal-ish positive ticket sizes via exp of gaussian amt = max(1.0, round((2.71828 ** random.gauss(3.0, 0.6)) + random.uniform(-5, 5), 2)) ch = random.choice(channels) rec = {"userId": u, "ts": ts, "amount": float(amt), "channel": ch} transactions.append(rec) transactions_by_user[u].append(rec) # Ensure transactions per user are time-sorted for deterministic windowing for u in users: transactions_by_user[u].sort(key=lambda r: r["ts"]) # ----------------------------- # Feature registry (governance) # ----------------------------- def build_feature_registry(): owner_default = "risk-ml@company.example" owner_growth = "growth-ml@company.example" features = [] # Base features features.append({ "name": "rolling_sum_7d", "description": "Sum of transaction amounts in the past 7 days inclusive of cutoff", "owner": owner_default, "freshness_sla": "PT1H", # ISO 8601 duration (1 hour) "window": "P7D", "source": "synthetic:memory" }) features.append({ "name": "txn_count_7d", "description": "Count of transactions in the past 7 days inclusive of cutoff", "owner": owner_default, "freshness_sla": "PT1H", "window": "P7D", "source": "synthetic:memory" }) features.append({ "name": "avg_ticket_7d", "description": "Average ticket size over the past 7 days (sum/count; 0 if no txns)", "owner": owner_growth, "freshness_sla": "PT1H", "window": "P7D", "source": "synthetic:memory" }) for ch in channels: features.append({ "name": f"channel_freq_{ch}_7d", "description": f"Frequency (proportion) of transactions via {ch} in past 7 days; 0 if no txns", "owner": owner_growth, "freshness_sla": "PT1H", "window": "P7D", "source": "synthetic:memory" }) registry = { "feature_store": "in-memory-demo", "defaults": { "point_in_time_policy": "use records with ts <= cutoff, window [cutoff-7d, cutoff]", "missing_policy": "emit zeros for counts/sums/frequencies; avg=0 if no txns", "time_zone": "UTC", }, "features": features } return registry registry = build_feature_registry() # ----------------------------- # Feature computation (point-in-time safe) # ----------------------------- WINDOW_SEC = 7 * 24 * 3600 def compute_features_for_user(user_id: str, cutoff: int): rows = transactions_by_user.get(user_id, []) lo = cutoff - WINDOW_SEC # Filter only events within [lo, cutoff], explicitly excluding future ts > cutoff window_rows = [r for r in rows if (r["ts"] <= cutoff and r["ts"] >= lo)] count = len(window_rows) total = sum(r["amount"] for r in window_rows) avg = (total / count) if count > 0 else 0.0 ch_counts = {ch: 0 for ch in channels} for r in window_rows: ch_counts[r["channel"]] += 1 ch_freqs = {ch: (ch_counts[ch] / count) if count > 0 else 0.0 for ch in channels} # Freshness (age) per feature = cutoff - newest event actually used if count > 0: last_ts = max(r["ts"] for r in window_rows) age_sec = cutoff - last_ts else: last_ts = None age_sec = None # no data in window vec = { "userId": user_id, "asOfTs": cutoff, "asOfIso": to_iso(cutoff), "rolling_sum_7d": round(total, 2), "txn_count_7d": count, "avg_ticket_7d": round(avg, 4), } for ch in channels: vec[f"channel_freq_{ch}_7d"] = round(ch_freqs[ch], 6) # Per-feature ages; same age for all window-derived features in this demo ages = { "rolling_sum_7d": age_sec, "txn_count_7d": age_sec, "avg_ticket_7d": age_sec, } for ch in channels: ages[f"channel_freq_{ch}_7d"] = age_sec meta = { "cutoff": cutoff, "cutoffIso": to_iso(cutoff), "windowStart": lo, "windowStartIso": to_iso(lo), "lastEventTsUsed": last_ts, "lastEventIso": to_iso(last_ts) if last_ts is not None else None, } return vec, ages, meta # Retrieval function required by task def retrieve_feature_vector(user_id: str, cutoff: int): return compute_features_for_user(user_id, cutoff) # ----------------------------- # Example vectors and freshness report # ----------------------------- users_sorted = sorted(users) first5 = users_sorted[:5] example_vectors = [] # Collect age stats across all users for a max-age freshness report age_by_feature = defaultdict(list) for u in users: _, ages, _ = retrieve_feature_vector(u, cutoff_ts) for k, v in ages.items(): age_by_feature[k].append(v) freshness_report = [] for feat, vals in age_by_feature.items(): # max age among non-None; if all None, keep None vals_non_none = [v for v in vals if v is not None] max_age = max(vals_non_none) if vals_non_none else None freshness_report.append({"feature": feat, "maxAgeSec": max_age}) for u in first5: vec, ages, meta = retrieve_feature_vector(u, cutoff_ts) example_vectors.append({"userId": u, "vector": vec, "ages": ages, "meta": meta}) notes = ( "Point-in-time safety: features are computed using only events with ts <= cutoff, " "and within the rolling 7-day window [cutoff-7d, cutoff]. Any transactions after the cutoff are ignored, " "preventing label leakage. Governance is demonstrated via the registry with owners and freshness SLAs." ) result = { "registry": registry, "exampleVectors": example_vectors, "freshnessReport": sorted(freshness_report, key=lambda d: d["feature"]), "notes": notes } print(json.dumps(result, indent=2)) PY python /workspace/mini_feature_store.py"}

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9.
